title: Cross-Validation for NMF Rank Selection
text: many factors data really ? Training loss always decreases kk increases — adding factors can improve fit training data, even extra factors fitting noise. makes train loss useless selecting optimal rank. RcppML solves speckled holdout cross-validation: randomly mask fraction individual matrix entries, fit NMF model remaining entries, evaluate reconstruction error held-entries. Unlike row column holdout, speckled masking preserves full structure every sample every feature. optimal rank kk test loss curve forms “elbow” — decreasing sharply true rank flattening thereafter.

title: Cross-Validation for NMF Rank Selection
text: Cross-validation triggered passing vector k: Return value: data.frame class nmfCrossValidate columns: Use plot(cv_result) built-test-loss-vs-rank curve.
code: nmf(data, k = c(2, 4, 6, 8, 10),     test_fraction = 0.1, cv_seed = 1:3,     mask_zeros = FALSE, patience = 5, ...)

title: Cross-Validation for NMF Rank Selection
text: mask_zeros = FALSE (default): entries (including zeros) can held . Tests full-matrix reconstruction. Appropriate dense non-sparse data. mask_zeros = TRUE: nonzero entries can held . Zeros treated “unobserved,” “zero-valued.” Appropriate recommendation data sparse ecological surveys zero means “seen” rather “measured zero.”

title: Cross-Validation for NMF Rank Selection
text: entry (,j)(, j), include test set probability test_fraction. entries form training set. preserves matrix structure — every row column retains entries training.

title: Cross-Validation for NMF Rank Selection
text: Plot test loss rank look elbow: point test loss transitions steep decrease (capturing real structure) plateau increase (overfitting noise). elbow, model underfitting. , ’s fitting noise.

title: Cross-Validation for NMF Rank Selection
text: Different cv_seed values produce different holdout masks different NMF initializations. Averaging test loss across replicates smooths variability sources. Use 2–3 replicates stable results.

what: Example 1: Recovering True Rank from Synthetic Data
title: Cross-Validation for NMF Rank Selection
text: generate matrix exactly 5 true factors ask cross-validation find elbow. Mean test loss per rank (3 replicates). Optimal k = 2. Cross-validation correctly identifies k=5 optimal rank — rank used generate synthetic data. Test loss drops sharply k=2 k=5, flattens, indicating additional structure beyond 5 factors.  clear elbow k=5 confirms speckled holdout CV recovers true rank. individual replicate points (dots) cluster tightly, showing 3 replicates suffice stable estimates matrix size.
code: sim <- simulateNMF(300, 150, k = 5, noise = 0.3, seed = 42) cv <- nmf(sim$A, k = 2:10, test_fraction = 0.1, cv_seed = 1:3,           tol = 1e-3, maxit = 50) # Compute mean test loss per rank agg <- aggregate(test_mse ~ k, data = cv, FUN = mean) agg$se <- aggregate(test_mse ~ k, data = cv, FUN = function(x) sd(x) / sqrt(length(x)))$test_mse optimal_k <- agg$k[which.min(agg$test_mse)]  knitr::kable(   data.frame(     k = agg$k,     `Mean Test Loss` = round(agg$test_mse, 6),     `SE` = round(agg$se, 6),     check.names = FALSE   ),   caption = paste0("Mean test loss per rank (3 replicates). Optimal k = ", optimal_k, ".") ) plot(cv) +   labs(title = "Cross-Validation: Test Loss vs. Rank (Synthetic Data, True k = 5)") +   theme_minimal()

what: Example 2: Sparse Ecological Data with mask_zeros
title: Cross-Validation for NMF Rank Selection
text: hawaiibirds dataset contains 183 bird species × 1,183 grid cells Hawaiian bird surveys. highly sparse — species absent cells. mask_zeros comparison hawaiibirds. Dense optimal k = 8, Sparse optimal k = 12.  two modes operate different scales: mask_zeros = FALSE tests full-matrix reconstruction (including predicting zeros), mask_zeros = TRUE tests prediction observed species counts. ecological survey data zeros mean “species detected,” mask_zeros = TRUE focuses evaluation ecologically meaningful predictions.
code: data(hawaiibirds)  ranks <- c(3, 5, 8, 12, 16) cv_dense <- nmf(hawaiibirds, k = ranks, test_fraction = 0.1,                 mask_zeros = FALSE, cv_seed = 1:3, tol = 1e-3, maxit = 50) cv_sparse <- nmf(hawaiibirds, k = ranks, test_fraction = 0.1,                  mask_zeros = TRUE, cv_seed = 1:3, tol = 1e-3, maxit = 50) agg_dense <- aggregate(test_mse ~ k, data = cv_dense, FUN = mean) agg_sparse <- aggregate(test_mse ~ k, data = cv_sparse, FUN = mean)  opt_dense <- agg_dense$k[which.min(agg_dense$test_mse)] opt_sparse <- agg_sparse$k[which.min(agg_sparse$test_mse)]  comparison <- data.frame(   k = ranks,   `Dense Test Loss` = round(agg_dense$test_mse, 6),   `Sparse Test Loss` = round(agg_sparse$test_mse, 4),   check.names = FALSE ) knitr::kable(   comparison,   caption = paste0("mask_zeros comparison on hawaiibirds. Dense optimal k = ",                     opt_dense, ", Sparse optimal k = ", opt_sparse, ".") ) cv_dense$mode <- "mask_zeros = FALSE" cv_sparse$mode <- "mask_zeros = TRUE" cv_both <- rbind(cv_dense, cv_sparse)  agg_both <- aggregate(test_mse ~ k + mode, data = cv_both, FUN = mean)  ggplot(cv_both, aes(x = k, y = test_mse, color = mode)) +   geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 2) +   geom_line(data = agg_both, aes(x = k, y = test_mse, color = mode), linewidth = 1) +   scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +   scale_x_continuous(breaks = ranks) +   labs(     title = "Cross-Validation: mask_zeros Comparison (Hawaiian Birds)",     x = "Rank (k)", y = "Test Loss", color = "Mode"   ) +   theme_minimal()

title: Cross-Validation for NMF Rank Selection
text: Using synthetic data, compare different holdout fractions test affect rank selection stability. Effect test_fraction rank selection (synthetic data, true k = 5). three fractions identify optimal rank, confirming robustness speckled holdout CV. Higher test fractions give precise estimates (lower SE) leave less data training. matrices 10,000+ nonzero entries, 5–10% reliable default.
code: fractions <- c(0.05, 0.1, 0.2) fraction_results <- lapply(fractions, function(frac) {   cv_res <- nmf(sim$A, k = 2:10, test_fraction = frac, cv_seed = 1:3,                 tol = 1e-3, maxit = 50)   agg <- aggregate(test_mse ~ k, data = cv_res, FUN = mean)   se <- aggregate(test_mse ~ k, data = cv_res, FUN = function(x) sd(x) / sqrt(length(x)))$test_mse   data.frame(     `Test Fraction` = frac,     `Optimal k` = agg$k[which.min(agg$test_mse)],     `Min Test Loss` = round(min(agg$test_mse), 6),     `Mean SE` = round(mean(se), 6),     check.names = FALSE   ) })  knitr::kable(   do.call(rbind, fraction_results),   caption = "Effect of test_fraction on rank selection (synthetic data, true k = 5).",   row.names = FALSE )

title: Cross-Validation for NMF Rank Selection
text: Distribution-aware CV: Use non-MSE loss functions count data. See Distributions vignette. Recommendation data: mask_zeros = TRUE essential rating prediction. See Recommendation Systems vignette. Robust factorization: Combine CV-selected rank consensus_nmf() stable factors across multiple runs. See Clustering vignette.

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: Standard NMF minimizes mean squared error, implicitly assuming Gaussian noise constant variance. assumption fails many real datasets: count data (gene expression, species surveys) variance proportional mean; heavy-tailed data extreme outliers dominate MSE objective; data excess zeros far beyond single distribution predicts (e.g., single-cell RNA-seq dropout). RcppML unifies distribution-specific NMF via Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares (IRLS): NMF iteration, least squares subproblem re-weighted match chosen distribution’s variance function. result better fit, interpretable factors, statistically principled modeling.

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: Use loss parameter specify error distribution:
code: nmf(data, k, loss = "gp", ...)

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: dispersion parameter controls dispersion estimated:

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: data excess zeros beyond chosen distribution predicts:
code: nmf(data, k, loss = "gp", zi = "row", ...)

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: auto_nmf_distribution(data, k, distributions, criterion) — fit multiple distributions, compare via AIC/BIC score_test_distribution(data, model, powers) — score test variance power without refitting diagnose_zero_inflation(data, model, threshold) — test excess zeros diagnose_dispersion(data, model) — recommend dispersion granularity

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: distribution assumes specific relationship variance mean: V(μ)=μpV(\mu) = \mu^p. Gaussian (p=0) constant variance. Poisson-family (p=1) variance proportional mean. Gamma (p=2) variance proportional mean squared. correct assumption determines residuals weighted — high-mean entries get downweighted count data, matching natural heteroscedasticity.

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: NMF iteration, IRLS computes weights wij=1/V(μ̂ij)w_{ij} = 1/V(\hat{\mu}_{ij}) solves weighted NNLS problem. iterative reweighting converges maximum likelihood estimate chosen distribution.

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: ZI mixture model decomposes observation : P(X=0)=π+(1−π)⋅f(0|μ)P(X=0) = \pi + (1-\pi) \cdot f(0|\mu). EM algorithm alternates estimating zero-inflation probabilities π\pi updating NMF factors — capturing dropout structural zeros base distribution explain.

what: Example 1: Distribution Auto-Selection on Count Data
title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: hawaiibirds dataset contains species frequency counts Hawaiian bird surveys — overdispersed count data Gaussian assumptions inappropriate. Distribution comparison hawaiibirds (BIC criterion). Best: mse.  BIC selects count-based distribution MSE, confirming bird count data mean-dependent variance. Gaussian assumption underestimates variance high-count sites, inflating effective number parameters needed good fit.
code: data(hawaiibirds) result <- auto_nmf_distribution(hawaiibirds, k = 8,                                  distributions = c("mse", "gp", "nb"),                                  criterion = "bic", seed = 42,                                  maxit = 30) comp <- result$comparison comp_display <- data.frame(   Distribution = comp$distribution,   NLL = round(comp$nll, 1),   df = comp$df,   AIC = round(comp$aic, 1),   BIC = round(comp$bic, 1),   Selected = ifelse(comp$selected, "***", "") ) knitr::kable(   comp_display,   caption = paste0("Distribution comparison on hawaiibirds (BIC criterion). Best: ", result$best, ".") ) ggplot(comp, aes(x = distribution, y = bic, fill = selected)) +   geom_col(width = 0.6) +   scale_fill_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "grey70", "TRUE" = "steelblue"), guide = "none") +   labs(title = "BIC Comparison Across Distributions (Hawaiian Birds)",        x = "Distribution", y = "BIC (lower is better)") +   theme_minimal()

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: score test evaluates variance-power family without refitting — fast diagnostic determine distribution matches data’s variance structure. Score test results. Best power: p = 1 (gp). power smallest |T||T| best matches observed variance-mean relationship. power near 1 (Poisson/GP family) indicates mean-proportional variance, power 2 (Gamma) indicates variance growing mean-squared. bird count data, score test confirms variance function best characterizes data’s noise structure.
code: model_base <- nmf(hawaiibirds, k = 8, seed = 42, tol = 1e-3, maxit = 30) scores <- score_test_distribution(hawaiibirds, model_base) score_df <- scores$scores score_df$T_stat <- round(score_df$T_stat, 4) score_df$abs_T <- round(score_df$abs_T, 4) knitr::kable(   score_df,   caption = paste0("Score test results. Best power: p = ", scores$best_power,                     " (", scores$best_distribution, ").") ) if (!is.null(scores$nb_diagnostic)) {   nb_msg <- if (scores$nb_diagnostic$overdispersed) {     "Substantial overdispersion detected (T_NB > 0.1). NB or GP may be preferable to Poisson."   } else {     "No strong overdispersion beyond Poisson detected."   } }

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: Single-cell RNA-seq data extreme sparsity “dropout” zeros beyond count distribution predicts. use pbmc3k, representative subset (8,000 genes × 500 cells) stored compressed SPZ raw bytes. subset 89.5% zeros — far single count distribution can explain. Zero-inflation diagnostics pbmc3k subset. Reconstruction error: GP vs. GP zero-inflation. Adding col-wise zero-inflation reduces reconstruction error 10.5%, confirming excess zeros single-cell data structural (gene dropout) rather sampling noise.
code: data(pbmc3k) tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".spz") writeBin(pbmc3k, tmp) counts <- st_read(tmp)  # Subset for speed: 500 genes × 500 cells counts_sub <- counts[1:min(500, nrow(counts)), 1:min(500, ncol(counts))] sparsity_pct <- round(100 * (1 - Matrix::nnzero(counts_sub) / prod(dim(counts_sub))), 1) model_gp <- nmf(counts_sub, k = 8, loss = "gp", seed = 42, tol = 1e-3, maxit = 30) zi_diag <- diagnose_zero_inflation(counts_sub, model_gp) zi_summary <- data.frame(   Metric = c("Excess Zero Rate", "Zero-Inflation Detected", "Recommended ZI Mode"),   Value = c(     round(zi_diag$excess_zero_rate, 4),     as.character(zi_diag$has_zi),     zi_diag$zi_mode   ) ) knitr::kable(zi_summary, caption = "Zero-inflation diagnostics on pbmc3k subset.") if (zi_diag$has_zi && zi_diag$zi_mode != "none") {   model_zi <- nmf(counts_sub, k = 8, loss = "gp", zi = zi_diag$zi_mode,                   seed = 42, tol = 1e-3, maxit = 30)      loss_gp <- evaluate(model_gp, counts_sub, loss = "mse")   loss_zi <- evaluate(model_zi, counts_sub, loss = "mse")   improvement <- round(100 * (1 - loss_zi / loss_gp), 1)      loss_comp <- data.frame(     Model = c("GP", paste0("GP + ZI (", zi_diag$zi_mode, ")")),     `MSE` = round(c(loss_gp, loss_zi), 4),     check.names = FALSE   )   knitr::kable(loss_comp, caption = "Reconstruction error: GP vs. GP with zero-inflation.") }

title: Statistical Distributions for NMF
text: Rank selection distributions: Cross-validate loss = "gp" "nb". See Cross-Validation vignette. Factor interpretation: Combine distribution-aware NMF consensus clustering. See Clustering vignette. Core NMF mechanics: Gaussian/MSE NMF fundamentals, see NMF Fundamentals.

title: Factorization Graphs, Guides, and Semi-Supervised Learning
text: Real-world problems rarely fit pattern “one matrix, one factorization.” Multi-modal data (e.g., gene expression + chromatin accessibility) sharing samples demands shared latent space. Prior knowledge — class labels, reference patterns — steer factors rather ignored. RcppML’s factor_net() treats factorization directed graph composable blocks. Combined guides inject domain knowledge directly optimization, enables workflows NMF package offers: classification-augmented embeddings, semi-supervised learning, multi-modal integration, automated hyperparameter search.

title: Factorization Graphs, Guides, and Semi-Supervised Learning
text: factor_config(maxit, tol, loss, test_fraction, ...) — global settings W(L1, guide, ...) / H(L1, guide, ...) — per-factor overrides regularization guides factor_net(inputs, output, config) — compile graph fit(net) — execute; returns factor_net_result per-layer access via result$layer_name

title: Factorization Graphs, Guides, and Semi-Supervised Learning
text: Guides add soft penalty biases factors toward target structure: minh≥0∥Gh−b∥2+λ∥h−t∥2\min_{h \geq 0} \|Gh - b\|^2 + \lambda\|h - t\|^2 tt guide target, recomputed iteration. guide_classifier: labels must 0-indexed integers; use -1 unlabeled samples.

title: Factorization Graphs, Guides, and Semi-Supervised Learning
text: layer_fn — function function(p) builds output layer parameter list p params — named list vectors search (e.g., list(k = c(3, 5, 10))) Returns ranked parameter combinations mean test loss
code: cross_validate_graph(inputs, layer_fn, params, config, reps = 3,                      strategy = c("grid", "random"), seed = 42L)

title: Factorization Graphs, Guides, and Semi-Supervised Learning
text: factor_net layer performs factorization. Layers can share factors (multi-modal), stacked (deep), branch (multi-task). engine optimizes layers jointly. Guide mechanics: guide_classifier computes current centroid class H matrix every iteration. biases factors toward class structure without overriding data-driven learning. lambda increases, factors shift purely data-driven purely label-driven. Semi-supervised: Labels -1 ignored guide. Labeled samples steer embedding; unlabeled samples follow data alone.

what: Example 1: Graph NMF Equivalence to nmf()
title: Factorization Graphs, Guides, and Semi-Supervised Learning
text: simplest factorization graph single input feeding single NMF layer — identical calling nmf() directly. factor_net produces equivalent results plain nmf() graph API produces identical results. value emerges multi-modal, guided, hierarchical architectures.
code: data(aml)  # Direct nmf() direct <- nmf(aml, k = 6, seed = 42, maxit = 100)  # Equivalent factor_net inp <- factor_input(aml, name = "chromatin") out <- nmf_layer(inp, k = 6, name = "nmf1") config <- factor_config(maxit = 100, seed = 42) net <- factor_net(inp, out, config) graph_result <- fit(net)  # Compute MSE from graph result factors (loss field reports total, not mean) W_gr <- graph_result$nmf1$W H_gr <- graph_result$nmf1$H d_gr <- graph_result$nmf1$d mse_graph <- mean((as.matrix(aml) - W_gr %*% diag(d_gr) %*% H_gr)^2)  equiv_table <- data.frame(   Method = c("nmf()", "factor_net"),   MSE = c(evaluate(direct, aml), mse_graph),   Iterations = c(direct@misc$iter, graph_result$nmf1$iterations),   check.names = FALSE ) knitr::kable(equiv_table, digits = 5,              caption = "factor_net produces equivalent results to plain nmf()")

title: Factorization Graphs, Guides, and Semi-Supervised Learning
text: Guides steer NMF factors toward class structure, producing embeddings jointly optimized reconstruction classification. trade-objectives becomes visible guidance strength increases. Guided NMF: reconstruction-classification trade-across lambda  clean data like Golub, even unsupervised NMF separates cancer types well. value guidance visible factor structure — guided factors align tightly class boundaries, matters downstream interpretation noisier datasets unsupervised separation fail. trade-table shows strong guidance (λ=5\lambda = 5) degrades reconstruction substantially.
code: data(golub) labels_factor <- attr(golub, "cancer_type") labels <- as.integer(labels_factor) - 1L  # 0 = ALL, 1 = AML  # Lambda sweep: from unsupervised to strongly guided lambdas <- c(0, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0) models <- lapply(lambdas, function(lam) {   if (lam == 0) {     nmf(t(golub), k = 3, seed = 42, maxit = 100)   } else {     nmf(t(golub), k = 3, guides = guide_classifier(labels, lambda = lam),         seed = 42, maxit = 100)   } })  # Reconstruction MSE for each mse_vals <- sapply(models, function(m) evaluate(m, t(golub)))  # Classification accuracy (kNN embedding classifier) set.seed(42) test_idx <- sort(sample(38, 11)) acc_vals <- sapply(models, function(m) {   classify_embedding(t(m@h), labels, test_idx = test_idx, k = 3)$accuracy })  comp <- data.frame(   Lambda = lambdas,   Accuracy = acc_vals,   `Reconstruction MSE` = mse_vals,   `MSE Increase (%)` = round(100 * (mse_vals / mse_vals[1] - 1), 1),   check.names = FALSE ) knitr::kable(comp, digits = c(1, 3, 0, 1),              caption = "Guided NMF: reconstruction-classification trade-off across lambda") # Scatter plot: Factor 1 vs Factor 2 for unsupervised vs. strongly guided scatter_data <- rbind(   data.frame(F1 = models[[1]]@h[1, ], F2 = models[[1]]@h[2, ],              Cancer = labels_factor, Model = "Unsupervised (λ = 0)"),   data.frame(F1 = models[[4]]@h[1, ], F2 = models[[4]]@h[2, ],              Cancer = labels_factor, Model = "Guided (λ = 1.0)") )  ggplot(scatter_data, aes(x = F1, y = F2, color = Cancer)) +   geom_point(size = 2.5) +   scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +   facet_wrap(~ Model, scales = "free") +   theme_minimal() +   labs(x = "Factor 1", y = "Factor 2",        title = "NMF embeddings: unsupervised vs. guided")

title: Factorization Graphs, Guides, and Semi-Supervised Learning
text: fraction samples labels, guided NMF propagates label structure shared low-dimensional space. Semi-supervised NMF partial labels Even half samples labeled, guided NMF achieves classification accuracy close fully supervised case, demonstrating effective semi-supervised learning shared latent space.
code: # Mask 50% of labels as unlabeled (-1) partial_labels <- labels set.seed(42) partial_labels[sample(length(labels), length(labels) %/% 2)] <- -1L  model_semi <- nmf(t(golub), k = 3,                   guides = guide_classifier(partial_labels, lambda = 1.0),                   seed = 42, maxit = 100)  eval_semi <- classify_embedding(t(model_semi@h), labels, test_idx = test_idx, k = 3) eval_unsup <- classify_embedding(t(models[[1]]@h), labels, test_idx = test_idx, k = 3) eval_guided <- classify_embedding(t(models[[4]]@h), labels, test_idx = test_idx, k = 3)  semi_table <- data.frame(   Model = c("Unsupervised", "Semi-supervised (50% labeled)", "Fully guided"),   Accuracy = c(eval_unsup$accuracy, eval_semi$accuracy, eval_guided$accuracy),   `Macro F1` = c(eval_unsup$macro_f1, eval_semi$macro_f1, eval_guided$macro_f1),   check.names = FALSE ) knitr::kable(semi_table, digits = 3,              caption = "Semi-supervised NMF with partial labels")

title: Factorization Graphs, Guides, and Semi-Supervised Learning
text: two data modalities share samples, factor_shared() discovers common latent space explains simultaneously. Shared factorization recovers common latent structure Shared factorization discovers common latent structure across two feature spaces, matching true generating factors high cosine similarity.
code: # Simulate two views of the same 100 samples sim1 <- simulateNMF(200, 100, k = 5, noise = 0.3, seed = 42) sim2 <- simulateNMF(150, 100, k = 5, noise = 0.3, seed = 43)  inp1 <- factor_input(sim1$A, name = "view1") inp2 <- factor_input(sim2$A, name = "view2") shared_inp <- factor_shared(inp1, inp2) shared <- nmf_layer(shared_inp, k = 5, name = "shared") config_mm <- factor_config(maxit = 100, seed = 42) net_mm <- factor_net(list(inp1, inp2), shared, config_mm) result_mm <- fit(net_mm)  # Compare learned H to true H from each view H_learned <- result_mm$shared$H cos_sim <- function(a, b) {   sum(a * b) / (sqrt(sum(a^2)) * sqrt(sum(b^2))) }  # Match factors using bipartiteMatch on cosine distance cost_mat <- matrix(0, 5, 5) for (i in 1:5) {   for (j in 1:5) {     cost_mat[i, j] <- 1 - cos_sim(H_learned[i, ], sim1$h[j, ])   } } match_result <- bipartiteMatch(cost_mat) matched_sims <- sapply(1:5, function(i) {   j <- match_result$assignment[i] + 1L  # 0-indexed to 1-indexed   cos_sim(H_learned[i, ], sim1$h[j, ]) })  modal_table <- data.frame(   `Learned Factor` = 1:5,   `Matched True Factor` = match_result$assignment + 1L,   `Cosine Similarity` = matched_sims,   check.names = FALSE ) knitr::kable(modal_table, digits = 3,              caption = "Shared factorization recovers common latent structure")

title: Factorization Graphs, Guides, and Semi-Supervised Learning
text: cross_validate_graph() automates grid search rank regularization strength. Top 5 parameter combinations mean test loss Grid search 9 parameter combinations identifies optimal rank L1 strength, balancing reconstruction accuracy overfitting.
code: data(aml) inp_cv <- factor_input(aml, name = "aml") layer_fn <- function(p) nmf_layer(inp_cv, k = p$k, L1 = c(p$L1, 0), name = "nmf_cv") params <- list(k = c(3, 5, 8), L1 = c(0, 0.01, 0.1)) config_cv <- factor_config(test_fraction = 0.1, cv_seed = 42, maxit = 50)  cv <- cross_validate_graph(inp_cv, layer_fn, params, config_cv,                            reps = 2, seed = 42, verbose = FALSE)  top5 <- head(cv$summary, 5) knitr::kable(top5, digits = 5,              caption = "Top 5 parameter combinations by mean test loss")

title: Getting Started with RcppML
text: RcppML decomposes nonnegative matrix AA low-rank factors ≈W⋅diag(d)⋅HA \approx W \cdot \text{diag}(d) \cdot H, WW captures features, HH captures sample loadings, dd gives per-factor scale. Built Rcpp Eigen OpenMP parallelism optional CUDA GPU support, RcppML designed large sparse matrices common genomics, recommender systems, image analysis. Key capabilities include: NMF coordinate-descent Cholesky NNLS solvers SVD / PCA optional non-negativity L1 constraints Cross-validation via speckled holdout automatic rank selection Distribution-aware losses: Gaussian, Poisson, Generalized Poisson, Negative Binomial, Gamma, Inverse Gaussian, Tweedie — plus zero-inflation Regularization: L1, L2, L21, angular, graph Laplacian, upper bounds Consensus clustering divisive clustering (dclust) Composable factorization graphs via factor_net() StreamPress .spz format --core computation GPU acceleration via CUDA

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: NMF’s core computation iterative matrix multiplication — dominant cost iteration computing Gram matrices (HHTH H^T, WTWW^T W) right-hand-side products (AHTA H^T, WTAW^T ). dense linear algebra operations map directly onto GPU hardware, thousands cores perform matrix multiplies parallel. RcppML’s GPU backend uses NVIDIA CUDA cuBLAS (dense operations) cuSPARSE (sparse matrix–dense matrix products) accelerate NMF 5–50× depending matrix size, density, GPU hardware. GPU optional — code falls back CPU transparently CUDA unavailable.

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: Two functions check GPU availability runtime: gpu_available() probes CUDA GPUs first call caches result session. Use gpu_available(force_recheck = TRUE) re-probe driver updates.
code: library(RcppML)  # Check if a CUDA GPU is available and the GPU library is loaded gpu_available() #> [1] TRUE  # Get device details gpu_info() #>   device   name total_mb  free_mb #> 1      0  GPU 0    32510    31200

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: nmf() function dispatches GPU via resource parameter: parameters (loss, L1, L2, mask_zeros, test_fraction, etc.) work identically GPU CPU. Results numerically equivalent within floating-point tolerance.
code: library(Matrix) A <- rsparsematrix(10000, 5000, density = 0.05)  # Auto-dispatch (uses GPU if available, otherwise CPU) model <- nmf(A, k = 20, seed = 42)  # Force CPU even when GPU is available model_cpu <- nmf(A, k = 20, resource = "cpu", seed = 42)  # Force GPU (errors if unavailable) model_gpu <- nmf(A, k = 20, resource = "gpu", seed = 42)

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: compute backend determined (highest priority first): resource parameter nmf() svd() RCPPML_RESOURCE environment variable options(RcppML.gpu = TRUE/FALSE) Auto-detection (GPU available, else CPU)
code: # Environment variable (session-wide) Sys.setenv(RCPPML_RESOURCE = "cpu")  # R option (session-wide) options(RcppML.gpu = FALSE)

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: large datasets stored .spz files, sp_read_gpu() reads file directly GPU memory, bypassing CPU entirely: gpu_sparse_matrix handle stores opaque device pointers CSC arrays GPU HBM. Passing nmf() avoids CPU--GPU transfer occurs using regular dgCMatrix.
code: # Read .spz directly into GPU device memory (zero CPU allocation) gpu_handle <- sp_read_gpu("large_dataset.spz") #> GPU sparse matrix: 30000 x 20000, nnz = 12000000, device 0  # Run NMF on GPU-resident data model <- nmf(gpu_handle, k = 30, seed = 42)  # Free GPU memory when done (optional — GC handles this automatically) sp_free_gpu(gpu_handle)

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: GPU-unsupported feature requested, RcppML falls back CPU automatically error.

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: GPU acceleration universally faster. benefit depends problem characteristics: GPU excels : Large matrices (> 5,000 rows columns): GPU kernel launch overhead amortized many floating-point operations. Moderate high rank (k≥10k \geq 10): Gram matrix NNLS solves scale O(k2)O(k^2), providing work saturate GPU cores. Many iterations: one-time cost transferring data GPU memory amortized across iterations. CPU may faster : Small matrices (< 1,000 elements per dimension): GPU kernel launch latency dominates computation time. low rank (k<5k < 5): little work keep GPU cores busy. Ultra-sparse data (> 99.5% zeros): Sparse operations higher GPU launch overhead relative actual computation.

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: benchmarks compare NVIDIA V100S (32 GB HBM2) dual Intel Xeon Gold (48 CPU cores). NMF run 100 iterations tol = 0 force full iteration count. Representative NMF benchmarks: CPU vs. GPU (V100S) Dense matrices benefit cuBLAS achieves near-peak FLOPS dense GEMM. Sparse matrices see lower speedups due cuSPARSE launch overhead, still provide substantial acceleration large problems.

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: simplest path GPU acceleration — code changes beyond resource = "gpu":
code: library(RcppML)  # Verify GPU is available gpu_available() #> [1] TRUE gpu_info() #>   device                      name total_mb free_mb #> 1      0  NVIDIA A100-SXM4-80GB     81920   79500  # Create a moderately large sparse matrix library(Matrix) A <- rsparsematrix(10000, 5000, density = 0.05)  # GPU NMF — same API, add resource="gpu" system.time(model_gpu <- nmf(A, k = 20, resource = "gpu", seed = 42)) #>    user  system elapsed #>   0.120   0.045   1.850  # CPU for comparison system.time(model_cpu <- nmf(A, k = 20, resource = "cpu", seed = 42)) #>    user  system elapsed #>  18.200   0.350  18.600  # Verify numerical equivalence all.equal(model_gpu@d, model_cpu@d, tolerance = 1e-5) #> [1] TRUE

what: Example 2: Streaming GPU NMF from StreamPress
title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: datasets large CPU memory, load .spz directly onto GPU:
code: # Read .spz directly into GPU memory — zero CPU-side matrix allocation gpu_handle <- sp_read_gpu("path/to/large_scRNAseq.spz")  # Run NMF entirely on GPU model <- nmf(gpu_handle, k = 30, loss = "gp", resource = "gpu",              seed = 42, maxit = 200)  # Extract factors (transferred back to CPU automatically) head(model@w[, 1]) #> [1] 0.00342 0.01205 0.00000 0.00891 0.00054 0.01133  # Free GPU memory when done sp_free_gpu(gpu_handle)

what: Example 3: CPU vs. GPU Timing Pattern
title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: template benchmarking GPU speedup data:
code:
library(RcppML) library(Matrix)  # Load or generate your data data <- rsparsematrix(20000, 10000, density = 0.03)  # Warm up: first call may include library loading overhead invisible(nmf(data[1:100, 1:100], k = 5, resource = "gpu", maxit = 5))  # Time CPU cpu_time <- system.time(   model_cpu <- nmf(data, k = 20, resource = "cpu", seed = 42, maxit = 100) )  # Time GPU gpu_time <- system.time(   model_gpu <- nmf(data, k = 20, resource = "gpu", seed = 42, maxit = 100) )  cat(sprintf("CPU: %.1f seconds
GPU: %.1f seconds
Speedup: %.1fx
",             cpu_time[3], gpu_time[3], cpu_time[3] / gpu_time[3]))

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: GPU shared library built separately R package using nvcc.

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: NVIDIA GPU compute capability ≥\geq 7.0 (Volta newer) CUDA Toolkit ≥\geq 12.x (cuBLAS cuSPARSE included)

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: Adapt -gencode flags GPU architecture: CUDA architecture flags GPU family
code: # 1. Load CUDA on your system module load cuda/12.8.1   # HPC example  # 2. Build the GPU shared library cd RcppML/src/ nvcc -O3 \   -gencode arch=compute_80,code=sm_80 \   -gencode arch=compute_90,code=sm_90 \   -std=c++17 -Xcompiler -fPIC \   --shared \   -I../inst/include \   -o ../inst/lib/RcppML_gpu.so \   gpu_bridge.cu \   -lcublas -lcusparse -lcuda  # 3. Verify Rscript -e 'library(RcppML); cat("GPU:", gpu_available(), "\n")' #> GPU: TRUE

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: RcppML searches RcppML_gpu.locations (order): Working directory: inst/lib/ (development) Working directory: src/ (build output) Package install directory: lib/ Working directory root

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: NMF GPU memory usage approximately: Memory≈nnz×12+(m+n)×k×8 bytes\text{Memory} \approx \text{nnz} \times 12 + (m + n) \times k \times 8 \text{ bytes} nnz number non-zeros, mm nn matrix dimensions, kk rank. 30,000 × 20,000 matrix 5% density k=30k = 30, roughly 3.6 GB — well within 32 GB GPU.

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: Request GPU resources SLURM-managed clusters:
code: # Interactive GPU session salloc --partition=gpu --gres=gpu:1 --cpus-per-task=8 --mem=32G --time=02:00:00  # Batch job #!/bin/bash #SBATCH --partition=gpu #SBATCH --gres=gpu:1 #SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8 #SBATCH --mem=32G #SBATCH --time=04:00:00  module load r/4.5.2 cuda/12.8.1  Rscript -e ' library(RcppML) cat("GPU available:", gpu_available(), "\n") print(gpu_info())  data(pbmc3k) tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".spz") writeBin(pbmc3k, tmp) counts <- st_read(tmp) model <- nmf(counts, k = 20, resource = "gpu", seed = 42) cat("Loss:", model@misc$loss, "\n") '

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: Common causes: CUDA drivers installed outdated: Run nvidia-smi check. GPU compute capability < 7.0: Pre-Volta GPUs supported. RcppML_gpu.found: Check library search path . Architecture mismatch: Library compiled sm_90 won’t work V100 (sm_70).
code: gpu_available(force_recheck = TRUE)  # Force re-probe #> [1] FALSE

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: GPU computation uses double precision (FP64). Single precision support planned future release enable faster performance consumer GPUs, fewer FP64 units data-center GPUs.

title: GPU-Accelerated Computing
text: API, different backend: resource = "gpu" change needed. loss functions, regularization, cross-validation work identically. 5–50× speedups large matrices, highest gains dense data high rank. Direct disk--GPU via sp_read_gpu() avoids CPU memory bottlenecks large datasets. Automatic fallback: GPU unavailable feature CPU-, RcppML falls back silently — code changes needed. See StreamPress vignette .spz file format details, NMF Fundamentals vignette comprehensive NMF parameter documentation.

title: Image Decomposition: Learning Visual Parts
text: Lee & Seung’s 1999 Nature paper demonstrated NMF learns parts-based representations images — decomposing faces eyes, noses, mouths rather holistic “eigenfaces” produced PCA. key insight nonnegativity forces factors add features rather cancel . factor captures localized “part,” every image nonneg combination parts. PCA components positive negative values, producing global modes (illumination, expression) require cancellation reconstruct specific images. NMF factors strictly nonneg — one recognizable building block. vignette makes distinction visually concrete.

title: Image Decomposition: Learning Visual Parts
text: Generates 32×32 stick figure images 4 limbs, 4 positions (true rank = 16). return_factors = TRUE, returns true W H matrices quantitative validation.
code: simulateSwimmer(n_images = 256, style = c("stick", "gaussian"),                 sigma = 1.5, noise = 0, seed = NULL, return_factors = FALSE)

title: Image Decomposition: Learning Visual Parts
text: Images stored rows (n_images × n_pixels), pixels columns NMF: transpose pixels rows, images columns → nmf(t(images), k) W columns factor images: matrix(model@w[, k], nrow_pixels, ncol_pixels)

title: Image Decomposition: Learning Visual Parts
text: bipartiteMatch(cost_matrix) — Hungarian algorithm optimal 1:1 factor correspondence. Returns 0-indexed $assignment.

title: Image Decomposition: Learning Visual Parts
text: NMF model ≈WHA \approx W H (pixels × images), column wkw_k W “part image” row hkh_k H encodes images contain part. nonnegativity constraints wik≥0w_{ik} \geq 0, hkj≥0h_{kj} \geq 0 mean image built summing parts — like assembling face shapes. PCA’s unconstrained components capture variance-maximizing directions (global illumination, expression), mix positive negative values. NMF parts capture localized features (eyes, nose, forehead). NMF interpretable per factor, though PCA may achieve lower reconstruction error per component.

title: Image Decomposition: Learning Visual Parts
text: swimmer dataset gold standard testing parts-based learning. image 32×32 stick figure fixed torso four limbs various positions. true rank 16 (4 limbs × 4 positions ). Top 8 learned factors matched ground truth (cosine similarity) NMF recovers swimmer factors high cosine similarity. Light L1 regularization helps isolate limb position cleanly.  factor captures specific limb position — torso base plus individual arm leg configurations. NMF decomposed images generative parts.
code: sim <- simulateSwimmer(256, style = "gaussian", sigma = 1.5, seed = 42,                        return_factors = TRUE) A <- t(sim$A)  # 1024 pixels x 256 images  model_swim <- nmf(A, k = 16, seed = 42, maxit = 100, L1 = c(0.1, 0)) # Match learned factors to ground truth # sim$h is 16 x 1024 (true factor images as rows) # model@w is 1024 x 16 (learned factor images as columns) cost <- matrix(0, 16, 16) for (i in 1:16) {   for (j in 1:16) {     a <- model_swim@w[, i]     b <- sim$h[j, ]     cost[i, j] <- 1 - sum(a * b) / (sqrt(sum(a^2)) * sqrt(sum(b^2)))   } } match_swim <- bipartiteMatch(cost)  # Cosine similarities for matched pairs cos_sims <- sapply(1:16, function(i) {   j <- match_swim$assignment[i] + 1L   a <- model_swim@w[, i]   b <- sim$h[j, ]   sum(a * b) / (sqrt(sum(a^2)) * sqrt(sum(b^2))) })  match_table <- data.frame(   `Factor` = 1:8,   `Cosine Similarity` = sort(cos_sims, decreasing = TRUE)[1:8],   check.names = FALSE ) knitr::kable(match_table, digits = 3,              caption = "Top 8 learned factors matched to ground truth (cosine similarity)") # 4x4 grid of all 16 learned factors plot_data <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:16, function(fi) {   vals <- model_swim@w[, fi]   grid <- expand.grid(x = 1:32, y = 1:32)   grid$value <- vals   grid$factor <- paste("Factor", fi)   grid }))  ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = x, y = y, fill = value)) +   geom_raster() +   scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "black") +   scale_y_reverse() +   facet_wrap(~ factor, ncol = 4) +   theme_minimal() +   theme(axis.text = element_blank(), axis.ticks = element_blank(),         axis.title = element_blank(), legend.position = "none",         strip.text = element_text(size = 8)) +   ggtitle("16 learned swimmer factors (32 x 32 each)")

title: Image Decomposition: Learning Visual Parts
text: Olivetti faces dataset contains 400 grayscale images (64×64) 40 subjects. Applying NMF reveals interpretable facial parts.  factor highlights different facial region — capture eye areas, others noses mouths, others forehead hair patterns. nonneg building blocks combine additively reconstruct face dataset.
code: data(olivetti) img_shape <- attr(olivetti, "image_shape")  # c(64, 64) A_faces <- t(olivetti[1:200, ])  # 4096 pixels x 200 images  model_faces <- nmf(A_faces, k = 15, seed = 42, maxit = 100) nrow_px <- img_shape[1] ncol_px <- img_shape[2]  face_data <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:15, function(fi) {   vals <- model_faces@w[, fi]   grid <- expand.grid(x = 1:ncol_px, y = 1:nrow_px)   grid$value <- vals   grid$factor <- paste("Factor", fi)   grid }))  ggplot(face_data, aes(x = x, y = y, fill = value)) +   geom_raster() +   scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "black") +   scale_y_reverse() +   facet_wrap(~ factor, ncol = 5) +   theme_minimal() +   theme(axis.text = element_blank(), axis.ticks = element_blank(),         axis.title = element_blank(), legend.position = "none",         strip.text = element_text(size = 8)) +   ggtitle("15 NMF facial parts from Olivetti faces")

what: Example 3: NMF vs PCA — Parts vs Eigenfaces
title: Image Decomposition: Learning Visual Parts
text: clearest way see nonnegativity matters compare NMF parts PCA eigenfaces data.  PCA eigenfaces show global patterns positive negative values — first component captures mean illumination, subsequent ones capture global expression modes cancellation. NMF parts show localized features strictly nonneg values — recognizable subset face (eyes, nose, jaw, forehead). NMF components interpretable “building blocks” faces, directly illustrating parts-based learning nonnegativity enables.
code: # PCA on the same data (6 components for comparison) pca_model <- pca(A_faces, k = 6, seed = 42) nmf_model <- nmf(A_faces, k = 6, seed = 42, maxit = 100) # Build comparison data make_image_df <- function(mat_cols, ncol_factors, method, nrow_px, ncol_px) {   do.call(rbind, lapply(1:ncol_factors, function(fi) {     vals <- mat_cols[, fi]     grid <- expand.grid(x = 1:ncol_px, y = 1:nrow_px)     grid$value <- vals     grid$factor <- paste("Component", fi)     grid$method <- method     grid   })) }  pca_data <- make_image_df(pca_model@u, 6, "PCA (eigenfaces)", nrow_px, ncol_px) nmf_data <- make_image_df(nmf_model@w, 6, "NMF (parts)", nrow_px, ncol_px)  p_pca <- ggplot(pca_data, aes(x = x, y = y, fill = value)) +   geom_raster() +   scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#2166ac", mid = "white", high = "#b2182b", midpoint = 0) +   scale_y_reverse() +   facet_wrap(~ factor, ncol = 6) +   theme_minimal() +   theme(axis.text = element_blank(), axis.ticks = element_blank(),         axis.title = element_blank(), legend.position = "none",         strip.text = element_text(size = 7)) +   ggtitle("PCA eigenfaces (diverging: blue = negative, red = positive)")  p_nmf <- ggplot(nmf_data, aes(x = x, y = y, fill = value)) +   geom_raster() +   scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "black") +   scale_y_reverse() +   facet_wrap(~ factor, ncol = 6) +   theme_minimal() +   theme(axis.text = element_blank(), axis.ticks = element_blank(),         axis.title = element_blank(), legend.position = "none",         strip.text = element_text(size = 7)) +   ggtitle("NMF parts (nonneg: white = zero, black = high)")  p_pca / p_nmf

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: can decompose nonnegative matrix interpretable parts? Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF) answers finding additive, parts-based representations: factor captures distinct pattern, weights nonneg, factors combine constructively. Unlike PCA, components can cancel , NMF factors always additive — making directly interpretable “parts” data. NMF widely used topic modeling, image decomposition, gene expression analysis, recommendation systems. RcppML provides high-performance implementation distinctive ≈W⋅diag(d)⋅HA \approx W \cdot \text{diag}(d) \cdot H factorization, diagonal scaling dd makes factors comparable ordered importance.

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: Key parameters: Initialization options: Normalization options:
code: nmf(data, k, tol = 1e-4, maxit = 100, seed = NULL,     init = c("random", "lanczos", "irlba"),     norm = c("L1", "L2", "none"),     L1 = c(0, 0), L2 = c(0, 0),     sort_model = TRUE, nonneg = c(TRUE, TRUE),     loss = "mse", threads = 0, verbose = FALSE,     on_iteration = NULL, ...)

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: nmf() function returns S4 object class nmf slots accessed via $: model$w — m×km \times k feature loading matrix (W) model$h — k×nk \times n sample embedding matrix (H) model$d — length-kk diagonal scaling vector dim(model) — returns c(m, n, k) head(model, n) — first nn factors model[] — subset specific factor indices

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: prod(model) — reconstructs W⋅diag(d)⋅HW \cdot \text{diag}(d) \cdot H (dense matrix) evaluate(model, data) — reconstruction loss (MSE default) mse(model$w, model$d, model$h, data) — standalone MSE computation

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: align(model, ref) — reorder factors match reference model (Hungarian algorithm) sparsity(model) — fraction near-zero entries W H cosine(x, y) — column-wise cosine similarity matrices bipartiteMatch(cost_matrix) — Hungarian algorithm optimal factor pairing (returns 0-indexed $assignment)

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: tol controls convergence threshold. model converges maximum change factor (measured correlation distance) falls tol. maxit provides hard iteration cap. on_iteration accepts callback function(iter, train_loss, test_loss) called iteration.

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: NMF solves constrained optimization problem: minW≥0,H≥0∥−W⋅diag(d)⋅H∥F2\min_{W \geq 0,\, H \geq 0} \|- W \cdot \text{diag}(d) \cdot H\|_F^2 AA m×nm \times n nonneg matrix, WW m×km \times k, HH k×nk \times n, dd length-kk scaling vector.

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: RcppML uses alternating non-negative least squares: fix H, solve W; fix W, solve H. full iteration, columns W rows H normalized scales absorbed dd. diagonal scaling makes factors directly comparable ordered importance sort_model = TRUE.

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: Random initialization gives noisy starting point may require iterations. SVD-based methods (Lanczos, IRLBA) compute truncated SVD warm start, typically converging faster — especially well-conditioned data.

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: NMF solutions unique: different seeds lead different local optima. Always set seed reproducibility. robust factorizations across multiple random starts, use consensus_nmf() (see Clustering vignette).

what: Example 1: Recovering Known Factors from Synthetic Data
title: NMF Fundamentals
text: generate synthetic matrix 5 true factors test whether NMF can recover . compare learned factors ground truth, compute cosine similarity pair W columns. bipartiteMatch() function finds optimal one--one assignment (note: returns 0-indexed assignments): Per-factor cosine similarity learned true W columns. NMF recovers 5 planted factors high cosine similarity despite 30% Gaussian noise.  learned W matrix closely mirrors true factor structure: factor activates distinct subset features, pattern correspondence clear despite noise.
code: sim <- simulateNMF(300, 150, k = 5, noise = 0.3, seed = 42) model <- nmf(sim$A, k = 5, seed = 1, tol = 1e-5, maxit = 100) sim_cos <- cosine(model$w, sim$w) match_result <- bipartiteMatch(1 - sim_cos + 1e-10) assignment <- match_result$assignment + 1L  # convert to 1-indexed  per_factor_cos <- sapply(seq_len(5), function(i) {   sim_cos[i, assignment[i]] })  knitr::kable(   data.frame(     Factor = 1:5,     `Cosine Similarity` = round(per_factor_cos, 4),     check.names = FALSE   ),   caption = "Per-factor cosine similarity between learned and true W columns." ) # Prepare data for side-by-side comparison true_w <- sim$w[, assignment] learned_w <- model$w  # Build long-format data for ggplot make_heatmap_df <- function(mat, label) {   df <- expand.grid(Feature = seq_len(nrow(mat)), Factor = seq_len(ncol(mat)))   df$Value <- as.vector(mat)   df$Source <- label   df } hm_df <- rbind(   make_heatmap_df(true_w, "True W"),   make_heatmap_df(learned_w, "Learned W") ) hm_df$Source <- factor(hm_df$Source, levels = c("True W", "Learned W"))  ggplot(hm_df, aes(x = Factor, y = Feature, fill = Value)) +   geom_raster() +   facet_wrap(~Source) +   scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "inferno") +   labs(title = "True vs. Learned Feature Loadings (W)", x = "Factor", y = "Feature") +   theme_minimal() +   theme(axis.text.y = element_blank(), axis.ticks.y = element_blank())

what: Example 2: Gene Expression Factor Interpretation (AML Data)
title: NMF Fundamentals
text: aml dataset contains 824 genomic regions × 135 samples Acute Myeloid Leukemia ATAC-seq data. Sample subtypes stored attr(aml, "metadata_h")$category. identify top features per factor — genomic regions highest loadings: Top 5 features (highest W loadings) per factor. Now visualize H matrix (sample embeddings) columns grouped AML subtype:  H heatmap reveals clear subtype structure: certain factors activate preferentially within specific AML subtypes, indicating NMF captures biologically meaningful programs chromatin accessibility data.
code: data(aml) meta <- attr(aml, "metadata_h") model_aml <- nmf(aml, k = 6, seed = 42, tol = 1e-5) top_n <- 5 top_features <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:6, function(f) {   w_col <- model_aml$w[, f]   idx <- order(w_col, decreasing = TRUE)[1:top_n]   data.frame(     Factor = f,     Rank = 1:top_n,     Feature = idx,     Loading = round(w_col[idx], 4)   ) }))  knitr::kable(   top_features,   caption = "Top 5 features (highest W loadings) per factor.",   row.names = FALSE ) subtypes <- meta$category col_order <- order(subtypes) h_ordered <- model_aml$h[, col_order] subtypes_ordered <- subtypes[col_order]  h_df <- expand.grid(Factor = seq_len(nrow(h_ordered)), Sample = seq_len(ncol(h_ordered))) h_df$Weight <- as.vector(h_ordered) h_df$Subtype <- rep(subtypes_ordered, each = nrow(h_ordered))  ggplot(h_df, aes(x = Sample, y = factor(Factor), fill = Weight)) +   geom_raster() +   scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "magma") +   labs(     title = "NMF Sample Embeddings (H) by AML Subtype",     x = "Samples (grouped by subtype)", y = "Factor"   ) +   theme_minimal() +   theme(axis.text.x = element_blank(), axis.ticks.x = element_blank())

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: compare random vs. Lanczos initialization tracking loss per iteration using on_iteration callback: Convergence comparison: random vs. Lanczos initialization AML data.  Lanczos initialization starts better initial point (SVD-based warm start) typically reaches low loss fewer iterations, random initialization requires iterations reach comparable solution.
code: # Track loss over iterations via callback track_loss <- function() {   losses <- numeric(0)   callback <- function(iter, train_loss, test_loss) {     losses[iter] <<- train_loss   }   list(callback = callback, get = function() losses) }  tracker_random <- track_loss() model_random <- nmf(aml, k = 6, seed = 42, init = "random", tol = 1e-6,                     maxit = 80, on_iteration = tracker_random$callback)  tracker_lanczos <- track_loss() model_lanczos <- nmf(aml, k = 6, seed = 42, init = "lanczos", tol = 1e-6,                      maxit = 80, on_iteration = tracker_lanczos$callback)  loss_random <- tracker_random$get() loss_lanczos <- tracker_lanczos$get()  convergence_df <- rbind(   data.frame(Iteration = seq_along(loss_random), Loss = loss_random, Init = "Random"),   data.frame(Iteration = seq_along(loss_lanczos), Loss = loss_lanczos, Init = "Lanczos") ) knitr::kable(   data.frame(     Initialization = c("Random", "Lanczos"),     Iterations = c(length(loss_random), length(loss_lanczos)),     `Final Loss` = round(c(tail(loss_random, 1), tail(loss_lanczos, 1)), 4),     check.names = FALSE   ),   caption = "Convergence comparison: random vs. Lanczos initialization on AML data." ) ggplot(convergence_df, aes(x = Iteration, y = Loss, color = Init)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +   scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +   labs(     title = "Convergence: Random vs. Lanczos Initialization",     x = "Iteration", y = "Reconstruction Loss", color = "Initialization"   ) +   theme_minimal()

title: NMF Fundamentals
text: Rank selection: many factors use? See Cross-Validation vignette. Non-Gaussian data: Count data, ratings, overdispersed data need distribution-aware NMF. See Distributions vignette. Factor structure: Control sparsity, smoothness, factor properties. See Regularization vignette.

title: Building a Movie Recommendation Engine with NMF
text: Recommendation systems power impactful applications machine learning — Netflix suggestions Spotify playlists. core idea behind collaborative filtering simple: users agreed past agree future. Given sparse matrix user–item ratings, NMF discovers latent preference dimensions explain observed ratings predict unobserved ones. critical subtlety: zeros ratings matrix mean “rated,” “rated zero.” Standard NMF treats zeros targets reconstruct, wastes model capacity predicting absence. RcppML’s mask_zeros parameter solves restricting loss observed entries .

title: Building a Movie Recommendation Engine with NMF
text: primary interface nmf() mask_zeros = TRUE: Key parameters recommendation:
code: model <- nmf(data, k, mask_zeros = TRUE, seed = 42)

title: Building a Movie Recommendation Engine with NMF
text: Pass vector ranks k automatic cross-validation:
code: cv <- nmf(data, k = 2:20, test_fraction = 0.2, mask_zeros = TRUE, seed = 42) plot(cv)  # visualize test error vs. rank

title: Building a Movie Recommendation Engine with NMF
code: # Reconstruct the full predicted rating matrix Ahat <- model@w %*% diag(model@d) %*% model@h  # Evaluate model loss on data evaluate(model, data, loss = "mse")

title: Building a Movie Recommendation Engine with NMF
text: movielens 3,867 × 610 sparse ratings matrix (movies × users) ratings 1 5 stars. Genre metadata available via attr(movielens, "genres"), 19 × 3,867 binary matrix mapping movie genres.

title: Building a Movie Recommendation Engine with NMF
text: NMF decomposes ratings matrix ≈W⋅diag(d)⋅HA \approx W \cdot \text{diag}(d) \cdot H, WW (movies × k) captures latent movie attributes, HH (k × users) captures user preferences, dd scales dimension’s importance. predicted rating user jj movie ii : ̂ij=∑ℓ=1kWiℓdℓHℓj\hat{}_{ij} = \sum_{\ell=1}^{k} W_{\ell} \, d_\ell \, H_{\ell j}

title: Building a Movie Recommendation Engine with NMF
text: mask_zeros = FALSE, loss function sums entries: ℒ=∑,j(Aij−̂ij)2\mathcal{L} = \sum_{,j} (A_{ij} - \hat{}_{ij})^2 Since 95% entries zero (unrated), model spends effort predicting zeros rather learning actual preferences. mask_zeros = TRUE: ℒ=∑(,j)∈Ω(Aij−̂ij)2\mathcal{L} = \sum_{(,j) \\Omega} (A_{ij} - \hat{}_{ij})^2 Ω\Omega set observed (non-zero) ratings. focuses model signal matters recommendation.

title: Building a Movie Recommendation Engine with NMF
text: fit NMF MovieLens data interpret latent factors genre composition. column WW represents latent “taste dimension.” examining genres load heavily factor, can interpret preference axis captures.  column heatmap reveals coherent preference axis. Factors load heavily Action Sci-Fi Romance capture affinity action-oriented films. Factors dominated Drama Romance represent art-house character-driven taste profiles. NMF discovers genre clusters without genre labels input — rating patterns . Top 5 movies per latent factor (first 4 factors)
code: data(movielens) genres <- attr(movielens, "genres")  model <- nmf(movielens, k = 10, mask_zeros = TRUE,              L1 = c(0, 0.2), tol = 1e-5, maxit = 100, seed = 42,              verbose = FALSE) # Coerce pattern matrix to numeric for arithmetic genres_num <- as(genres, "dgCMatrix") * 1.0  # Compute genre loadings per factor: genres (19 x 3867) times W (3867 x 10) genre_factor <- as.matrix(genres_num %*% model@w) genre_counts <- Matrix::rowSums(genres_num) genre_factor_norm <- genre_factor / genre_counts  genre_df <- expand.grid(   Genre = rownames(genres),   Factor = paste0("F", seq_len(ncol(model@w))) ) genre_df$Loading <- as.vector(genre_factor_norm)  ggplot(genre_df, aes(x = Factor, y = Genre, fill = Loading)) +   geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +   scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "magma", direction = -1) +   labs(title = "Genre Signatures of Latent Taste Dimensions",        x = "Latent Factor", y = "") +   theme_minimal() +   theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)) # Top 5 movies for first 4 factors top_movies <- do.call(rbind, lapply(1:4, function(f) {   top_idx <- order(model@w[, f], decreasing = TRUE)[1:5]   movie_genres <- vapply(top_idx, function(idx) {     col <- as.logical(as.vector(genres_num[, idx]))     paste(rownames(genres)[col], collapse = ", ")   }, character(1))   data.frame(     Factor = paste0("F", f),     Movie = rownames(movielens)[top_idx],     Loading = round(model@w[top_idx, f], 3),     Genres = movie_genres,     stringsAsFactors = FALSE   ) }))  knitr::kable(top_movies, row.names = FALSE,              caption = "Top 5 movies per latent factor (first 4 factors)")

title: Building a Movie Recommendation Engine with NMF
text: Choosing right number latent factors critical. factors underfit (can’t capture genre nuances); many overfit (memorize noise individual ratings). RcppML’s built-cross-validation holds fraction observed ratings measures prediction error held-set. Cross-validation: prediction error held-ratings Test MSE decreases rank increases, showing additional factors capture real preference structure. gap train test error indicates generalization capacity — widening gap signals overfitting.
code: cv <- nmf(movielens, k = c(3, 5, 8, 10, 15, 20), test_fraction = 0.2,           mask_zeros = TRUE, tol = 1e-5, maxit = 100, seed = 42, verbose = FALSE)  knitr::kable(cv[, c("k", "train_mse", "test_mse")], digits = 3,              col.names = c("Rank (k)", "Train MSE", "Test MSE"),              caption = "Cross-validation: prediction error on held-out ratings")

title: Building a Movie Recommendation Engine with NMF
text: mask_zeros parameter fundamentally changes model optimizes. compare modes ranks illustrate difference. Cross-validation test error: masking zeros vs. results reveal two fundamentally different optimization targets: mask_zeros = TRUE: Test MSE scale squared rating errors (~7–10), test set contains actual ratings (values 1–5). Higher rank consistently improves predictions. mask_zeros = FALSE: Test MSE much smaller (~0.3) test set dominated zero entries trivially predicted. test error increases rank beyond k = 5, revealing classic overfitting — model spends capacity fitting rare non-zero entries expense predicting sea zeros. recommendation, mask_zeros = TRUE correct choice: evaluates model task actually performs — predicting users rate, predicting entries missing.
code: cv_no_mask <- nmf(movielens, k = c(3, 5, 8, 10, 15, 20), test_fraction = 0.2,                   mask_zeros = FALSE, tol = 1e-5, maxit = 100, seed = 42,                   verbose = FALSE)  compare_df <- data.frame(   k = cv$k,   `Test MSE (mask_zeros=TRUE)` = round(cv$test_mse, 3),   `Test MSE (mask_zeros=FALSE)` = round(cv_no_mask$test_mse, 4),   check.names = FALSE )  knitr::kable(compare_df, align = "r",              caption = "Cross-validation test error: masking zeros vs. not")

title: Building a Movie Recommendation Engine with NMF
text: mask_zeros = TRUE essential recommendation: zeros mean “unobserved,” “zero-rated.” Masking focuses model actual preferences. Rank selection via cross-validation prevents overfitting. test error curve reveals optimal complexity data. Genre analysis latent factors confirms NMF discovers interpretable preference dimensions rating patterns alone — genre labels needed. NNLS projection (via nnls()) enables real-time recommendation new users without refitting model. See Cross-Validation vignette automated rank selection test_fraction, Distributions vignette non-Gaussian loss functions ratings data.

title: Regularization and Constraints
text: Unregularized NMF often produces dense factors every feature loads every component. result hard interpret — factors overlap extensively capture redundant structure. Regularization gives control: sparse factors (active features per component), decorrelated factors (minimal redundancy components), smooth factors (respecting spatial network structure data). RcppML provides six distinct penalty mechanisms, applied independently W H.

title: Regularization and Constraints
text: L1 = c(w_penalty, h_penalty) — LASSO penalty, range [0, 1]. Drives small coefficients exactly zero, producing element-wise sparsity. L2 = c(w_penalty, h_penalty) — Ridge penalty, range ≥ 0. Shrinks large coefficients toward zero without producing exact zeros. L21 = c(w_penalty, h_penalty) — Group sparsity, range ≥ 0. Zeros entire rows columns W H, eliminating full features samples factor.

title: Regularization and Constraints
text: angular = c(w_penalty, h_penalty) — Orthogonality penalty, range ≥ 0. Penalizes correlation factor pairs, encouraging decorrelated components. graph_W, graph_H — Sparse graph Laplacian matrices (dgCMatrix). Penalizes differences connected nodes, enforcing spatial network smoothness. graph_lambda = c(w_strength, h_strength) — Controls strength graph regularization.

title: Regularization and Constraints
text: upper_bound = c(w_bound, h_bound) — Maximum coefficient value. Useful bounded data probabilities normalized scores. nonneg = c(TRUE, TRUE) — Nonnegativity toggle. Set FALSE allow negative coefficients (semi-NMF).

title: Regularization and Constraints
text: penalty parameters accept either single value (applied W H) length-2 vector c(w, h) independent control. Zero means penalty (default ). penalty modifies NNLS subproblem: L1 subtracts RHS (soft thresholding), L2 adds Gram diagonal (Tikhonov), angular adds -diagonal terms Gram (decorrelation), graph Laplacian adds Laplacian matrix Gram (smoothness).

title: Regularization and Constraints
text: NMF solves ≈W⋅diag(d)⋅HA \approx W \cdot \text{diag}(d) \cdot H alternating NNLS updates. penalty extends per-column subproblem: minh≥0∥Gh−b∥2+λ1∥h∥1+λ2∥h∥22\min_{h \geq 0} \|Gh - b\|^2 + \lambda_1 \|h\|_1 + \lambda_2 \|h\|^2_2 L1 (LASSO): Produces element-wise sparsity. Ideal factor activate subset features. L2 (Ridge): Shrinks coefficients smoothly. Prevents individual loadings dominating. L21 (Group LASSO): Zeros entire groups — useful feature selection across factors. Angular: Penalizes cos(wi,wj)\cos(w_i, w_j) factor pairs, pushing toward orthogonality. Graph Laplacian: Adds λtr(HLH⊤)\lambda \, \text{tr}(H L H^\top) L=D−AL = D - graph Laplacian. Connected nodes receive similar factor values.

what: Example 1: L1 Sparsity Spectrum on Swimmers
title: Regularization and Constraints
text: swimmer benchmark generates 32×32 images stick figures 4 limbs 4 positions (true rank = 16). fit NMF four L1 levels see sparsity affects factor interpretability. L1 sparsity vs. reconstruction tradeoff swimmer benchmark L1 = 0 (penalty), factors dense hard interpret. L1 increases, coefficients driven zero — factor isolates specific limb position. Beyond L1 = 0.5, factors become -sparse lose structure.  progression clear: L1 = 0 produces diffuse factors; L1 = 0.3 yields clean, localized limb positions; L1 = 0.5 begins -penalize.
code: sim <- simulateSwimmer(256, style = "gaussian", seed = 42, return_factors = TRUE) A <- t(sim$A)  # pixels (1024) x images (256)  l1_values <- c(0, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5) results <- lapply(l1_values, function(l1) {   nmf(A, k = 16, L1 = c(l1, 0), seed = 42, maxit = 50, verbose = FALSE) })  sparsity_table <- data.frame(   L1 = l1_values,   `W Sparsity (% zeros)` = sapply(results, function(m) round(100 * mean(m@w == 0), 1)),   `Reconstruction MSE` = sapply(results, function(m) round(evaluate(m, A), 5)),   check.names = FALSE ) knitr::kable(sparsity_table, digits = c(2, 1, 5),              caption = "L1 sparsity vs. reconstruction tradeoff on the swimmer benchmark") # Show one representative factor at each L1 level plot_data <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_along(l1_values), function(i) {   model <- results[[i]]   # Show 4 diverse factors   factor_ids <- c(1, 5, 9, 13)   do.call(rbind, lapply(factor_ids, function(fi) {     if (fi > ncol(model@w)) return(NULL)     vals <- model@w[, fi]     grid <- expand.grid(x = 1:32, y = 1:32)     grid$value <- vals     grid$factor <- paste("Factor", fi)     grid$L1 <- paste0("L1 = ", l1_values[i])     grid   })) }))  ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = x, y = y, fill = value)) +   geom_raster() +   scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "black") +   scale_y_reverse() +   facet_grid(L1 ~ factor) +   theme_minimal() +   theme(axis.text = element_blank(), axis.ticks = element_blank(),         axis.title = element_blank(), legend.position = "none",         strip.text = element_text(size = 8)) +   ggtitle("Swimmer factors at increasing L1 sparsity")

what: Example 2: Angular Decorrelation on Face Data
title: Regularization and Constraints
text: factors correlated, capture overlapping structure. Angular regularization penalizes pairwise correlation factors, producing distinct components. Angular regularization reduces inter-factor correlation  Angular regularization produces factors capture distinct facial features, substantially reduced pairwise correlation. reconstruction cost modest.
code: data(olivetti) A_faces <- t(olivetti[1:100, ])  # 4096 pixels x 100 images  baseline <- nmf(A_faces, k = 10, seed = 42, maxit = 50) decorr <- nmf(A_faces, k = 10, angular = c(0.5, 0), seed = 42, maxit = 50)  # Compute factor correlation matrices cor_base <- cor(baseline@w) cor_decorr <- cor(decorr@w)  # Mean absolute off-diagonal correlation mean_cor_base <- mean(abs(cor_base[upper.tri(cor_base)])) mean_cor_decorr <- mean(abs(cor_decorr[upper.tri(cor_decorr)]))  cor_table <- data.frame(   Method = c("Baseline (no penalty)", "Angular (0.5)"),   `Mean |correlation|` = c(mean_cor_base, mean_cor_decorr),   `Max |correlation|` = c(max(abs(cor_base[upper.tri(cor_base)])),                           max(abs(cor_decorr[upper.tri(cor_decorr)]))),   `Reconstruction MSE` = c(evaluate(baseline, A_faces),                            evaluate(decorr, A_faces)),   check.names = FALSE ) knitr::kable(cor_table, digits = 4,              caption = "Angular regularization reduces inter-factor correlation") # Build correlation heatmap data for both models make_cor_df <- function(cor_mat, label) {   n <- nrow(cor_mat)   grid <- expand.grid(Factor1 = 1:n, Factor2 = 1:n)   grid$correlation <- as.vector(cor_mat)   grid$method <- label   grid }  heatmap_data <- rbind(   make_cor_df(cor_base, "Baseline"),   make_cor_df(cor_decorr, "Angular (0.5)") )  ggplot(heatmap_data, aes(x = Factor1, y = Factor2, fill = correlation)) +   geom_raster() +   scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#2166ac", mid = "white", high = "#b2182b",                        midpoint = 0, limits = c(-1, 1)) +   facet_wrap(~ method) +   theme_minimal() +   theme(aspect.ratio = 1) +   labs(fill = "Correlation", title = "Factor correlation: Baseline vs. Angular regularization")

what: Example 3: Graph Laplacian for Spatial Smoothness
title: Regularization and Constraints
text: data spatial network structure, graph Laplacian regularization encourages connected nodes share similar factor values. build k-nearest-neighbor graph geographic coordinates Hawaiian bird survey grid cells use produce spatially coherent community patterns. Graph-regularized NMF produces spatially smoother factors Graph-regularized NMF produces spatially coherent community patterns — nearby grid cells load similarly factor, reflecting geographic structure bird communities. smoothness metric decreases substantially modest increase reconstruction error.
code: data(hawaiibirds) meta <- attr(hawaiibirds, "metadata_h")  # Build k-NN adjacency matrix from lat/lng coordinates coords <- as.matrix(meta[, c("lat", "lng")]) n <- nrow(coords) k_nn <- 10  # Compute pairwise distances and find k nearest neighbors dist_mat <- as.matrix(dist(coords)) adj <- Matrix::sparseMatrix(i = integer(0), j = integer(0), x = numeric(0),                             dims = c(n, n)) for (i in seq_len(n)) {   neighbors <- order(dist_mat[i, ])[2:(k_nn + 1)]  # exclude self   adj[i, neighbors] <- 1   adj[neighbors, i] <- 1  # symmetric }  # Graph Laplacian: L = D - A degree <- Matrix::rowSums(adj) laplacian <- Matrix::Diagonal(x = degree) - adj laplacian <- as(laplacian, "dgCMatrix")  # Fit with and without graph regularization baseline_g <- nmf(hawaiibirds, k = 6, seed = 42, maxit = 50) smooth_g <- nmf(hawaiibirds, k = 6, graph_H = laplacian,                 graph_lambda = c(0, 0.5), seed = 42, maxit = 50)  # Measure smoothness: mean squared difference between connected H values measure_smoothness <- function(model, adj_mat) {   H <- model@h   ij <- Matrix::which(adj_mat != 0, arr.ind = TRUE)   ij <- ij[ij[, 1] < ij[, 2], , drop = FALSE]  # upper triangle only   total <- 0   for (f in seq_len(nrow(H))) {     diffs <- H[f, ij[, 1]] - H[f, ij[, 2]]     total <- total + mean(diffs^2)   }   total / nrow(H) }  smooth_table <- data.frame(   Method = c("Baseline (no graph)", "Graph Laplacian (λ = 0.5)"),   `Reconstruction MSE` = c(evaluate(baseline_g, hawaiibirds),                            evaluate(smooth_g, hawaiibirds)),   `H Smoothness (mean Δ²)` = c(measure_smoothness(baseline_g, adj),                                 measure_smoothness(smooth_g, adj)),   check.names = FALSE ) knitr::kable(smooth_table, digits = 5,              caption = "Graph-regularized NMF produces spatially smoother factors")
