title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: R’s built-serialization formats (.rds, .rda) general-purpose inefficient sparse matrices. serialize entire CSC structure including slot metadata, inflates file sizes slows /O large datasets. StreamPress (.spz) columnar compressed format designed specifically sparse matrices. uses rANS entropy coding delta-encoded indices achieve 2–20× compression .rds files supporting random column access streaming reads. dense matrices, StreamPress v3 provides optional FP16, QUANT8, rANS compression codecs.

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: st_write() compresses sparse dgCMatrix .spz format: Returns invisible list compression statistics: raw_bytes, compressed_bytes, ratio, compress_ms.
code: stats <- st_write(x, path,   precision = "auto",         # "fp64", "fp32", "fp16", "quant8", or "auto"   include_transpose = TRUE,   # store CSC(A^T) for fast row access   delta = TRUE,               # delta prediction for row indices   value_pred = FALSE,         # value prediction for integer data   chunk_cols = NULL,          # columns per chunk (NULL = auto from chunk_bytes)   chunk_bytes = 64e6,         # target bytes per chunk   verbose = FALSE             # print compression statistics )

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: st_read() decompresses .spz file dgCMatrix: cols parameter enables partial reads — load columns need without decompressing entire file.
code: mat <- st_read(path,   cols = NULL,      # column indices to read (NULL = all)   reorder = TRUE,   # undo any row permutation   threads = 0       # number of threads (0 = all) )

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: st_info() reads header, decompression:
code: info <- st_info(path) # Returns: rows, cols, nnz, density_pct, file_bytes, raw_bytes, ratio, #          version, value_type, chunk_cols, num_chunks, ...

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: StreamPress v3 handles dense matrices optional compression codecs:
code: st_write_dense(x, path,   codec = "raw",              # "raw", "fp16", "quant8", "fp16_rans", "fp32_rans"   include_transpose = FALSE,  # store transposed panels   chunk_cols = 2048L          # columns per chunk ) mat <- st_read_dense(path)

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: StreamPress stores column independently CSC-like layout. design enables: Random column access: Read column jj without decompressing columns 1 j−1j-1. Streaming /O: Process columns chunks --core NMF datasets larger RAM. Parallel decompression: Independent columns can decoded concurrently.

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: column, StreamPress applies: Delta encoding row indices: Store differences consecutive indices rather absolute values. dense rows, deltas mostly 1 — tiny integers compress extremely well. rANS entropy coding: asymmetric numeral system approaches Shannon entropy bound, yielding near-optimal compression. Precision reduction (optional): Convert 64-bit doubles 32-bit floats, 16-bit half-precision, 8-bit quantized values entropy coding.

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: integer count data (UMI counts, ratings), fp32 fp16 lossless limits shown. quant8 maps values 256 equally-spaced buckets, introducing quantization error proportional data range.

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: write MovieLens ratings matrix StreamPress verify lossless recovery. MovieLens storage: R object vs. .rds vs. .spz .spz format preserves numeric values CSC structure exactly. Dimension names (row/column labels) stored .spz file ; use R attributes sidecar metadata .
code: data(movielens)  # Write to StreamPress spz_file <- tempfile(fileext = ".spz") stats <- st_write(movielens, spz_file, include_transpose = FALSE)  # Inspect the file info <- st_info(spz_file)  # Read back ml_back <- st_read(spz_file)  # Compare file sizes rds_file <- tempfile(fileext = ".rds") saveRDS(movielens, rds_file)  size_df <- data.frame(   Format = c("R object (in memory)", ".rds file", ".spz file"),   `Size (KB)` = c(     round(as.numeric(object.size(movielens)) / 1024, 1),     round(file.info(rds_file)$size / 1024, 1),     round(file.info(spz_file)$size / 1024, 1)   ),   check.names = FALSE )  knitr::kable(size_df, align = "lr",              caption = "MovieLens storage: R object vs. .rds vs. .spz") # Verify values are identical (dimnames may differ since SPZ doesn't store them) cat("Values identical:", identical(movielens@x, ml_back@x), "\n") #> Values identical: TRUE cat("Structure identical:", identical(movielens@i, ml_back@i) &&       identical(movielens@p, ml_back@p), "\n") #> Structure identical: TRUE

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: StreamPress v3 handles dense matrices optional compression codecs. AML dense matrix storage comparison
code: data(aml)  # Write dense matrix dense_file <- tempfile(fileext = ".spz") st_write_dense(aml, dense_file)  # Read back aml_back <- st_read_dense(dense_file)  # Compare sizes rds_dense <- tempfile(fileext = ".rds") saveRDS(aml, rds_dense)  dense_df <- data.frame(   Format = c("R object (in memory)", ".rds file", ".spz v3 file"),   `Size (KB)` = c(     round(as.numeric(object.size(aml)) / 1024, 1),     round(file.info(rds_dense)$size / 1024, 1),     round(file.info(dense_file)$size / 1024, 1)   ),   check.names = FALSE ) knitr::kable(dense_df, align = "lr",              caption = "AML dense matrix storage comparison") cat("Max absolute difference:", max(abs(aml - aml_back)), "\n") #> Max absolute difference: 2.98015e-08

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: Different precision modes trade file size numerical accuracy. write MovieLens data precision level measure error introduced. Precision vs. compression tradeoff (MovieLens ratings) integer rating data (values 1–5), fp64, fp32, fp16 lossless values fit within exact representation range format. quant8 introduces quantization error, mapping continuous value range 256 discrete buckets.
code: precisions <- c("fp64", "fp32", "fp16", "quant8") prec_results <- data.frame(   Precision = precisions,   `File Size (KB)` = NA_real_,   `Max Abs Error` = NA_real_,   Lossless = NA_character_,   check.names = FALSE,   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  for (i in seq_along(precisions)) {   f <- tempfile(fileext = ".spz")   st_write(movielens, f, precision = precisions[i], include_transpose = FALSE)   back <- st_read(f)   err <- max(abs(movielens@x - back@x))   prec_results$`File Size (KB)`[i] <- round(file.info(f)$size / 1024, 1)   prec_results$`Max Abs Error`[i] <- round(err, 6)   prec_results$Lossless[i] <- if (err == 0) "Yes" else "No" }  knitr::kable(prec_results, align = "lrrr",              caption = "Precision vs. compression tradeoff (MovieLens ratings)")

what: Example 4: Real-World Dataset — pbmc3k Single-Cell RNA-seq
title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: pbmc3k dataset ships StreamPress-compressed raw bytes — representative subset (8,000 genes × 500 cells) 10x Genomics PBMC 3k dataset, demonstrating SPZ enables shipping sparse datasets within CRAN’s tarball size limits. pbmc3k StreamPress file summary
code: # Load compressed bytes (not a matrix — raw bytes) data(pbmc3k)  # Write to temp file and inspect pbmc3k_file <- tempfile(fileext = ".spz") writeBin(pbmc3k, pbmc3k_file) pbmc_info <- st_info(pbmc3k_file)  pbmc_df <- data.frame(   Property = c("Rows (genes)", "Columns (cells)", "Non-zeros",                "Density", "SPZ file size", "Compression ratio"),   Value = c(     format(pbmc_info$rows, big.mark = ","),     format(pbmc_info$cols, big.mark = ","),     format(pbmc_info$nnz, big.mark = ","),     paste0(round(pbmc_info$density_pct, 1), "%"),     paste0(round(pbmc_info$file_bytes / 1024, 0), " KB"),     paste0(round(pbmc_info$ratio, 1), "x")   ),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) knitr::kable(pbmc_df, align = "lr", caption = "pbmc3k StreamPress file summary") # Load into R and run a quick NMF on a subset counts <- st_read(pbmc3k_file)  # Subset for speed: 500 most variable genes × all cells # Compute row variance efficiently: Var = E[x^2] - E[x]^2 n <- ncol(counts) row_means <- Matrix::rowMeans(counts) row_sq_means <- Matrix::rowMeans(counts^2) gene_var <- row_sq_means - row_means^2 top_genes <- order(gene_var, decreasing = TRUE)[1:500] counts_sub <- counts[top_genes, ]  model <- nmf(counts_sub, k = 5, seed = 42, tol = 1e-4, maxit = 50,              verbose = FALSE) cat("NMF complete: k =", ncol(model@w), ", loss =",     round(model@misc$loss, 2), "\n") #> NMF complete: k = 5 , loss = 3287246

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: datasets large fit memory, StreamPress enables streaming NMF processes matrix chunks directly disk: Streaming reads chunks columns .spz file, updates factor matrices incrementally, discards chunk loading next. enables NMF datasets 10–100× larger available RAM.
code: # Stream NMF from a large .spz file # The matrix is never fully loaded into RAM model <- nmf("/path/to/huge_matrix.spz", k = 20, streaming = TRUE,              maxit = 50, seed = 42)

title: StreamPress: High-Performance Sparse Matrix I/O
text: StreamPress achieves substantial compression R objects .rds files, near-instantaneous decompression random column access. Precision modes offer clear tradeoff: fp64 lossless preservation, fp32/fp16 lossless integer data, quant8 maximum compression controlled error. pbmc3k shipping pattern (raw bytes .rda → writeBin → st_read) enables distributing large datasets within CRAN’s tarball size limits. Streaming NMF .spz files enables analysis datasets larger available RAM. See GPU Acceleration vignette GPU-direct reads .spz, NMF Fundamentals vignette fitting NMF models loaded data.

title: SVD and PCA
text: Sometimes don’t need nonnegative parts — need axes capture variance. PCA SVD workhorses unconstrained dimensionality reduction: orthogonal, optimal mean squared error, applicable real-valued data. Unlike NMF, PCA components can mixed signs, allowing cancellation capturing signed contrasts (e.g., “high group , low group B”). RcppML’s svd() fast (Eigen-based), sparse-aware, regularizable. Use PCA data mixed signs want orthogonal axes. Use NMF want interpretable nonneg parts. Use sparse PCA want interpretable loadings PCA framework.

title: SVD and PCA
text: Returns S4 object class svd_pca slots: @u — m×km \times k score matrix (left singular vectors) @d — length-kk singular values @v — n×kn \times k loading matrix (right singular vectors) @misc — metadata (including $row_means centered)
code: svd(A, k = 10, tol = 1e-5, maxit = 200,     center = FALSE, scale = FALSE,     seed = NULL, threads = 0,     L1 = 0, L2 = 0, nonneg = FALSE,     method = "auto", resource = "auto", ...)

title: SVD and PCA
text: Convenience wrapper: pca(, k, ...) equivalent svd(, k, center = TRUE, ...). Row means stored model@misc$row_means.

title: SVD and PCA
text: sparse loadings, use L1 regularization directly:
code: # Sparse PCA: L1 penalty on loadings (v) svd(A, k = 5, center = TRUE, L1 = c(0, 0.1))  # Nonneg PCA: nonneg-constrained loadings svd(A, k = 5, center = TRUE, nonneg = TRUE)

title: SVD and PCA
text: variance_explained(model) — proportion variance per component predict(model, newdata) — project new data learned subspace reconstruct(model) — low-rank approximation original matrix dim(model) — returns c(m, n, k) model[] — subset specific components

title: SVD and PCA
text: truncated SVD finds rank-kk approximation ≈UΣVTA \approx U \Sigma V^T minimizes Frobenius norm residual. UU contains left singular vectors (scores), Σ\Sigma diagonal matrix singular values, VV contains right singular vectors (loadings).

title: SVD and PCA
text: PCA centers data first: Ac=−‾A_c = - \bar{}, computes SVD AcA_c. proportion variance explained component ii σi2/∥Ac∥F2\sigma_i^2 / \|A_c\|_F^2.

title: SVD and PCA
text: Adding L1 penalty loadings trades variance explained interpretability: loading fewer active features. Nonneg constraints produce parts-like loadings (similar NMF) maintaining orthogonal decomposition framework.

title: SVD and PCA
text: olivetti dataset contains 400 face images (40 subjects × 10 poses ) 400 × 4,096 matrix. row 64×64 grayscale face image. Variance explained first 10 principal components Olivetti faces. first component alone captures substantial fraction variance (overall brightness), diminishing returns later components. first 10 components together capture meaningful share total variance.  Component 1 captures average face brightness (illumination), later components capture increasingly localized features: head pose, expression, lighting direction. eigenfaces form orthogonal basis face space.
code: data(olivetti) img_shape <- attr(olivetti, "image_shape")  # c(64, 64)  # PCA: pixels as rows, images as columns model <- pca(t(olivetti), k = 10, seed = 42) ve <- variance_explained(model) knitr::kable(   data.frame(     Component = 1:10,     `Variance Explained` = round(ve, 4),     `Cumulative` = round(cumsum(ve), 4),     check.names = FALSE   ),   caption = "Variance explained by the first 10 principal components of Olivetti faces." ) # Reshape loadings (v columns) into face images n_show <- 6 face_list <- lapply(1:n_show, function(i) {   img <- matrix(model@v[, i], img_shape[1], img_shape[2])   df <- expand.grid(Row = 1:img_shape[1], Col = 1:img_shape[2])   df$Value <- as.vector(img)   df$Component <- paste("Component", i)   df }) face_df <- do.call(rbind, face_list)  ggplot(face_df, aes(x = Col, y = rev(Row), fill = Value)) +   geom_raster() +   facet_wrap(~Component, nrow = 2) +   scale_fill_gradient2(low = "blue", mid = "white", high = "red", midpoint = 0) +   labs(title = "Eigenfaces: First 6 Principal Components") +   theme_minimal() +   theme(     axis.text = element_blank(), axis.ticks = element_blank(),     axis.title = element_blank(), strip.text = element_text(size = 10)   ) +   coord_equal()

what: Example 2: Sparse PCA for Interpretable Loadings
title: SVD and PCA
text: Using aml dataset (824 genomic regions × 135 AML samples), compare standard PCA sparse PCA see L1 regularization concentrates loadings fewer features. Standard vs. Sparse PCA AML data (L1 = 0.2 loadings). Sparse PCA focuses component smaller set active features, trading modest variance substantially sparser loadings. makes individual components interpretable: one highlights compact set genomic regions rather spreading weight across features.  sparse PCA loadings concentrated zero large values, standard PCA loadings spread uniformly — illustrating sparsity-variance tradeoff.
code: data(aml) standard <- pca(aml, k = 5, seed = 42) sparse <- RcppML::svd(aml, k = 5, center = TRUE, L1 = c(0, 0.2), seed = 42) std_sparsity <- sapply(1:5, function(i) mean(abs(standard@v[, i]) < 1e-8)) sp_sparsity <- sapply(1:5, function(i) mean(abs(sparse@v[, i]) < 1e-8)) std_ve <- variance_explained(standard) sp_ve <- variance_explained(sparse)  comparison <- data.frame(   Component = 1:5,   `Std Var Explained` = round(std_ve, 4),   `Sparse Var Explained` = round(sp_ve, 4),   `Std Loading Sparsity` = round(std_sparsity, 3),   `Sparse Loading Sparsity` = round(sp_sparsity, 3),   check.names = FALSE ) knitr::kable(comparison, caption = "Standard vs. Sparse PCA on AML data (L1 = 0.2 on loadings).") loading_df <- rbind(   data.frame(     Value = abs(as.vector(standard@v[, 1:3])),     Component = rep(paste("Component", 1:3), each = nrow(standard@v)),     Method = "Standard PCA"   ),   data.frame(     Value = abs(as.vector(sparse@v[, 1:3])),     Component = rep(paste("Component", 1:3), each = nrow(sparse@v)),     Method = "Sparse PCA"   ) )  ggplot(loading_df, aes(x = Value, fill = Method)) +   geom_histogram(bins = 50, alpha = 0.6, position = "identity") +   facet_wrap(~Component, scales = "free_y") +   scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set1") +   labs(     title = "Loading Magnitude Distribution: Standard vs. Sparse PCA",     x = "|Loading|", y = "Count"   ) +   theme_minimal()

what: Example 3: Nonneg PCA vs. Standard PCA
title: SVD and PCA
text: Nonneg PCA constrains loadings nonneg, producing parts-like components similar NMF maintaining PCA framework. Three-way comparison PCA variants AML data. method offers distinct tradeoff: standard PCA maximizes variance explained, sparse PCA maximizes loading interpretability, nonneg PCA produces parts-like loadings maintaining orthogonality. Choose based whether need maximum fidelity (standard), feature selection (sparse), parts-based interpretation (nonneg).
code: nn <- RcppML::svd(aml, k = 5, center = TRUE, nonneg = TRUE, seed = 42)  nn_sparsity <- sapply(1:5, function(i) mean(abs(nn@v[, i]) < 1e-8)) nn_ve <- variance_explained(nn)  three_way <- data.frame(   Method = c("Standard PCA", "Sparse PCA (L1=0.2)", "Nonneg PCA"),   `Mean Var Explained` = round(c(mean(std_ve), mean(sp_ve), mean(nn_ve)), 4),   `Mean Loading Sparsity` = round(c(mean(std_sparsity), mean(sp_sparsity), mean(nn_sparsity)), 3),   check.names = FALSE ) knitr::kable(   three_way,   caption = "Three-way comparison of PCA variants on AML data." )

title: Copilot Instructions for RcppML Development
text: RcppML R package high-performance Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF) using Rcpp Eigen. codebase includes standard NMF cross-validation NMF support sparse matrices.

title: Copilot Instructions for RcppML Development
text: Critical Discovery: Different packages may compute -named metric differently. singlet’s test_error: Includes matrix entries (zeros + nonzeros) test set RcppML’s sparse=TRUE test loss: non-zero entries test set RcppML’s sparse=FALSE test loss: matrix entries (matches singlet) Lesson: comparing packages, always verify exact metric computation, just name. 26x difference “MSE” traced different test set definitions, bug.

title: Copilot Instructions for RcppML Development
text: recommendation systems (e.g., MovieLens ratings): - sparse=TRUE (mask_zeros=TRUE): predict existing ratings → appropriate recommendation - sparse=FALSE (mask_zeros=FALSE): Predict entries → measures full matrix reconstruction dense data (e.g., images, gene expression): - modes similar since entries non-zero - sparse=FALSE standard choice

title: Copilot Instructions for RcppML Development
text: Different packages use different random number generators: - singlet: Custom hash-based RNG speckled mask (seed.draw(j, , inv_density)) - RcppML: Unified RNG class reproducible seeds Lesson: Even seed, different RNG implementations produce different masks. Focus statistical equivalence, exact replication.

title: Copilot Instructions for RcppML Development
text: singlet CV: Crashes k > 16 datasets RcppML CV: Works tested ranks (k 64) Lesson: Always test edge cases (high rank, large datasets) benchmarking.

title: Copilot Instructions for RcppML Development
text: profiling NMF: 1. NNLS solver: O(k²m) O(k²n) per update 2. Gram matrix computation: O(k²n) H’H, O(k²m) W’W 3. Loss computation: Can O(nnz·k) O(m·n·k) depending mask_zeros 4. Memory access patterns: Column-major iteration faster sparse CSC matrices

title: Copilot Instructions for RcppML Development
text: Force full iterations: Use tol=1e-10 ensure maxit iterations run Multiple replicates: least 3 capture variance Warm-: First run may slower due JIT/cache effects Separate timing: Measure different phases (H update, W update, loss) independently

title: Copilot Instructions for RcppML Development

title: Copilot Instructions for RcppML Development
text: Sparse matrix format: RcppML expects dgCMatrix (CSC format). Convert (x, "dgCMatrix"). Factor scaling: RcppML uses W·diag(d)·H factorization. Don’t forget diagonal scaling. Transposed storage: Internal W stored k×m (transposed) cache efficiency. NA handling: Use mask="NA" parameter; C++ handle R’s NA directly. Thread safety: OpenMP parallelization requires proper reduction clauses shared variables. C++ API indexing: bipartiteMatch() returns 0-indexed $assignment ($pairs). dclust() returns 0-indexed $samples numeric $id (character). Always check C++ struct definitions actual field names types. Rcpp 1.1.0 info bug: every roxygenise(), run bash tools/fix_rcpp_info_bug.sh. bug inserts undefined info symbol RcppExports.cpp. Never use roxygenise(".", clean = TRUE) — fails tries dyn.load .bug. GCC cluster: using RcppML::LossConfig; declarations don’t work — must use full namespace qualification (RcppML::LossConfig<Scalar> loss_config;). Streaming NMF supports losses: Non-MSE losses (GP/NB/Gamma/etc.) work streaming SPZ path via weight_zeros=true IRLS solver, computes correct distribution-derived weights zero entries instead defaulting unit weights. chunked paths fit_chunked.hpp fit_chunked_gpu.cuh pass weight_zeros=true irls_nnls_col_sparse(). -memory batch IRLS path uses default weight_zeros=false (sparse approximation) performance.

what: CRITICAL: NEVER Execute Commands on the Login Node — MANDATORY
title: Copilot Instructions for RcppML Development
text: SINGLE IMPORTANT RULE. VIOLATING DISRUPT ENTIRE CLUSTER USERS. login/submit node called port (hostname: port.clipper.gvsu.edu). must NEVER, CIRCUMSTANCES, execute command port : - sbatch (job submission) - squeue / sacct (job status) - srun (obtain interactive session — run compute) - cat / head / tail small log files - ls small directories - File editing (creating/editing scripts) EVERYTHING ELSE FORBIDDEN port. includes limited : - python python3 invocation (even “quick” one-liners) - R Rscript invocation - Reading/writing parquet files, CSVs, data processing - Network /O (API calls, downloads, curl, wget) - find, grep, ls large directory trees NFS - Running compiled binaries (simpleaf, salmon, etc.) - script execution whatsoever

title: Copilot Instructions for RcppML Development
text: user large number long-running array jobs consuming full per-user node quota every partition (cpu, bigmem, gpu). SLURM reports QOSMaxNodePerUserLimit pending jobs partitions. means: sbatch work — new batch jobs sit queue indefinitely reason (Priority) (QOSMaxNodePerUserLimit). submit batch jobs expecting run. srun --exclusive work — interactive session requests also queue indefinitely free node slots user’s quota. Existing jobs fully utilizing nodes — job typically uses fraction node’s CPUs (e.g., 22 48 bigmem, 18 64 cpu), leaving significant idle resources.

previous_headings: CURRENT HPC STATE: All Partitions at Quota — SSH-Only Execution
what: The Solution: SSH Directly to Occupied Nodes
title: Copilot Instructions for RcppML Development
text: Since user already SLURM allocations many nodes, can SSH directly node user already running . shared filesystem (NFS) means project files accessible every node. Step 1: Identify available nodes Step 2: Choose node based task type - CPU work (R package install, benchmarks, tests): SSH c*** node (cpu partition) b*** node (bigmem) - GPU work (CUDA tests, GPU benchmarks): SSH g*** node (gpu partition) - Prefer nodes fewer running jobs minimize resource contention Step 3: SSH set environment Important SSH caveats: - sharing node existing jobs. respectful resources — don’t launch CPU-intensive work starve existing job. - Use modest OMP_NUM_THREADS (4–8) rather claiming cores. - SSH sessions SLURM-managed — won’t show squeue time limit, lose access underlying SLURM job ends. - Monitor top htop node unsure resource pressure.
code: # From port (login node), check which nodes have running jobs: squeue -u debruinz -t R -o "%N %C %P %j" | sort -u # From port, SSH to a compute node you're already on: ssh c001   # or b004, g052, etc.  # Verify you landed correctly: hostname   # Must NOT be "port"  # Set up environment: module load r/4.5.2 export OMP_NUM_THREADS=4   # Use a modest thread count to coexist with running jobs

what: How VS Code Agent Terminal Approval Works
title: Copilot Instructions for RcppML Development
text: agent runs terminal command, VS Code checks chat.tools.terminal.autoApprove .vscode/settings.json. setting maps first word command approval rule: "command": true → silently auto-approved, prompt ever shown "command": false → always blocked, requires manual approval matching rule → user prompted ; “Always Allow” creates session-scoped auto-approve Pattern matching first token command string: - ssh g051 'anything' → matches ssh - cd /mnt/... && git status → matches cd (git) - R ---save -e '...' → matches R Every unique first word lacks true rule = one user interruption. agent uses ssh, hostname, squeue, git, ls, cat, diff, find, wc, md5sum directly causes 10 separate approval prompts.

title: Copilot Instructions for RcppML Development
text: workspace includes .vscode/settings.json pre-approves safe commands: configuration, zero approval prompts needed normal agent workflows.
code: "chat.tools.terminal.autoApprove": {     "ssh": true,        // primary execution vehicle for compute nodes     "squeue": true,     // SLURM job status     "sacct": true,      // SLURM accounting     "srun": true,       // SLURM interactive     "sbatch": true,     // SLURM batch submit     "scancel": true,    // SLURM cancel     "R": true,          // R interpreter     "Rscript": true,    // R script runner     "hostname": true,   // node verification     "cat": true,        // log reading     "head": true,       // log reading     "tail": true,       // log reading     "ls": true,         // directory listing     "git": true,        // version control     "module": true,     // environment modules     "rm": false,        // BLOCKED — destructive     "kill": false,      // BLOCKED — destructive     ... }

title: Copilot Instructions for RcppML Development
text: Rule 1: Use VS Code tools instead terminal read-operations. require ZERO terminal commands ZERO approvals: Rule 2: compute-node work, start every command ssh. Since agent terminal runs port (login node), compute work must go SSH: Rule 3: commands whose first word pre-approved list, wrap ssh. must use diff, md5sum, wc, find, , make, python, executable: Since nodes share NFS, works file /mnt/home/. Rule 4: hostname check unnecessary SSH. old instruction said “run hostname command.” SSH, inherently know node ’re : - ssh c004 'command' → runs c004, guaranteed - need separate hostname verification step Rule 5: R wrappers multi-step compute workflows. build/install/test sequences, use R orchestrator inside SSH: create .R script run via ssh <node> 'Rscript path//script.R'.
code: # GOOD — uses pre-approved "ssh": ssh c004 'cd /mnt/home/debruinz/RcppML-2 && module load r/4.5.2 && Rscript test.R' ssh c004 'squeue -u debruinz -t R -o "%N %P" | tail -n+2 | sort -u' ssh c004 'cd /mnt/home/debruinz/RcppML-2 && git status --short | head -20' ssh c004 'hostname && whoami' ssh g051 'cd /mnt/home/debruinz/RcppML-2 && module load r/4.5.2 cuda/12.8.1 && Rscript bench.R'  # BAD — uses multiple executables, each could trigger approval if not pre-approved: cd /mnt/... && git status   # "cd" as first word for f in ...; do ... done   # "for" — NOT pre-approved, triggers prompt diff file1 file2            # "diff" — NOT pre-approved, triggers prompt md5sum file1 file2          # "md5sum" — NOT pre-approved, triggers prompt wc -l file                  # "wc" — NOT pre-approved, triggers prompt # Wrap in ssh so the first word is always "ssh" (pre-approved): ssh c004 'diff file1 file2' ssh c004 'md5sum file1 file2' ssh c004 'wc -l file' ssh c004 'for f in *.R; do echo "$f"; done' ssh c004 'cd /mnt/home/debruinz/RcppML-2 && module load r/4.5.2 && R --no-save -e "   devtools::document()   system(\"bash tools/fix_rcpp_info_bug.sh\")   devtools::install(quick = TRUE)   devtools::test() "'

what: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
text: RcppML R package high-performance Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF), truncated SVD/PCA, divisive clustering large sparse dense matrices. supports six statistical distributions via IRLS, cross-validation automatic rank selection, optional CUDA GPU acceleration, --core streaming datasets larger memory, composable graph DSL multi-modal deep NMF.

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
text: RcppML decomposes matrix lower-rank non-negative factors: ≈ W · diag(d) · H W (features × k) H (k × samples) columns/rows scaled unit sum via diagonal d, ensuring interpretable, scale-invariant factors. Key capabilities: Fast NMF alternating least squares, coordinate descent Cholesky NNLS, OpenMP parallelism via Eigen C++ library Six statistical distributions — Gaussian (MSE), Generalized Poisson, Negative Binomial, Gamma, Inverse Gaussian, Tweedie — fitted via Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares (IRLS) Cross-validation speckled holdout masks principled rank selection GPU acceleration via CUDA (cuBLAS/cuSPARSE) automatic fallback CPU Streaming NMF SparsePress (.spz) files datasets exceed available memory FactorNet graph API composable multi-modal, deep, branching NMF pipelines Truncated SVD five methods: deflation, Krylov, Lanczos, IRLBA, randomized Divisive clustering via recursive rank-2 factorizations

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
text: GPU support (optional): Requires CUDA Toolkit ≥ 11.0 installed system. See vignette("gpu-acceleration") build instructions.
code: # Stable release from CRAN install.packages("RcppML")  # Development version from GitHub (requires Rcpp and RcppEigen) devtools::install_github("zdebruine/RcppML")

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
code: library(RcppML)  # Load a built-in single-cell gene expression dataset data(pbmc3k)  # 32738 genes × 2700 cells sparse matrix  # Run NMF with rank k = 10 model <- nmf(pbmc3k, k = 10) model #> nmf model #>   k = 10 #>   w: 32738 x 10 #>   d: 10 #>   h: 10 x 2700  # Inspect factor loadings head(model@w) model@d model@h[, 1:5]  # Reconstruction error evaluate(model, pbmc3k)  # Project onto new data H_new <- predict(model, pbmc3k[, 1:100])  # Plot the model summary plot(model)

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
text: RcppML goes beyond standard Frobenius-norm NMF supporting distribution-appropriate loss functions via IRLS. critical count data (scRNA-seq, text mining) Gaussian assumptions wrong.

what: Example: Negative Binomial NMF for scRNA-seq
title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
code: data(pbmc3k)  # NB is the natural choice for scRNA-seq count data model_nb <- nmf(pbmc3k, k = 10, loss = "nb")  # Or use the distribution API with fine-grained control model_nb <- nmf(pbmc3k, k = 10,                 distribution = "nb",                 distribution_config = list(                   nb_size_init = 10,     # Initial size parameter r                   nb_size_max = 1e6      # Upper bound for r                 ))  # Automatic distribution selection via BIC auto <- auto_nmf_distribution(pbmc3k, k = 10) auto$best       # "nb" auto$comparison  # BIC/AIC table for each distribution

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
text: data excess zeros (e.g., droplet-based scRNA-seq), zero-inflated GP NB models estimate structural dropout probabilities separately count model:
code: # Zero-inflated Negative Binomial with per-row dropout model_zinb <- nmf(pbmc3k, k = 10, loss = "nb", zi = "row")  # Automatic zero-inflation detection model <- nmf(pbmc3k, k = 10, loss = "nb", zero_inflation = "auto")

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
text: Cross-validation uses speckled holdout mask — random subset matrix entries held training, test error computed entries. enables principled rank selection.
code: data(pbmc3k)  # Sweep k = 2 through 20, three replicates per rank cv <- nmf(pbmc3k, k = 2:20, test_fraction = 0.1, cv_seed = 1:3)  # Visualize test error vs rank plot(cv)  # Automatic rank selection (binary search) model_auto <- nmf(pbmc3k, k = "auto") model_auto@misc$rank_search$k_optimal

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
code: cv <- nmf(data, k = 2:15,           test_fraction = 0.1,      # 10% holdout           mask_zeros = TRUE,        # Only non-zero entries in test set (for sparse data)           patience = 5,             # Early stopping patience           cv_seed = c(42, 123, 7))  # Multiple seeds for replicates

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
text: RcppML supports comprehensive suite regularization methods can combined freely:

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
code: # Symmetric L1 on both W and H model <- nmf(data, k = 10, L1 = 0.1)  # Asymmetric: sparse H (embedding), dense W (loadings) model <- nmf(data, k = 10, L1 = c(0, 0.2))

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
code: model <- nmf(data, k = 10, L2 = c(0.01, 0.01))

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
text: L21-norm regularization drives entire rows W columns H toward zero, effectively performing automatic factor selection:
code: model <- nmf(data, k = 20, L21 = c(0.1, 0)) # Some factors in W will be entirely zeroed out

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
text: Encourages orthogonality factors:
code: model <- nmf(data, k = 10, angular = c(0.1, 0.1))

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
text: features samples known graph structure (e.g., gene regulatory network, spatial coordinates), graph regularization encourages connected nodes similar factor representations:
code: # gene_graph: m × m sparse adjacency matrix model <- nmf(data, k = 10, graph_W = gene_graph, graph_lambda = 0.5)  # Both feature and sample graphs model <- nmf(data, k = 10,              graph_W = gene_graph, graph_H = cell_graph,              graph_lambda = c(0.5, 0.3))

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
code: # Box constraint: all entries in W and H between 0 and 1 model <- nmf(data, k = 10, upper_bound = c(1, 1))

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
code: # Huber-type robustness (less sensitive to outliers) model <- nmf(data, k = 10, robust = TRUE)  # Custom Huber delta model <- nmf(data, k = 10, robust = 2.0)  # MAE (L1 loss) model <- nmf(data, k = 10, robust = "mae")

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
text: RcppML optionally uses CUDA GPU-accelerated NMF, delivering 10–20× speedups large matrices. GPU backend supports: - Standard NMF (sparse dense) - Cross-validation NMF - MSE loss (GPU-native), losses via automatic CPU fallback - OpenMP + CUDA hybrid execution - Zero-copy NMF sp_read_gpu() pre-loaded GPU data
code: # Check GPU availability gpu_available() #> [1] TRUE  gpu_info() #> $device_name: "NVIDIA A100" #> $total_memory_mb: 40960  # Sparse NMF on GPU model <- nmf(data, k = 20, resource = "gpu")  # Dense NMF on GPU model <- nmf(as.matrix(data), k = 20, resource = "gpu")  # Auto-dispatch (default): uses GPU if available, falls back to CPU model <- nmf(data, k = 20, resource = "auto")

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
text: datasets exceed available RAM, RcppML streams data SparsePress (.spz) compressed files — column-oriented binary format 10–20× compression via rANS entropy coding. Note: SparsePress format renamed StreamPress upcoming release.

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
code: library(RcppML) data(pbmc3k)  # Write sparse matrix to .spz file spz_path <- tempfile(fileext = ".spz") sp_write(pbmc3k, spz_path, include_transpose = TRUE)  # Read back into memory mat <- sp_read(spz_path) identical(dim(mat), dim(pbmc3k))  # TRUE  # File size comparison file.size(spz_path)  # Much smaller than RDS/RDA

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
text: Streaming NMF processes data column panels double-buffered asynchronous /O, maintaining O(m·k + n·k + chunk) memory regardless total matrix size.
code: # NMF directly from .spz file — data never fully loaded into memory model <- nmf(spz_path, k = 10)  # Streaming cross-validation cv <- nmf(spz_path, k = 2:15, test_fraction = 0.1)  # Streaming with non-MSE distributions model <- nmf(spz_path, k = 10, loss = "nb")

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
text: factor_net() API composes complex factorization pipelines simple building blocks. Multi-modal, deep, branching NMF networks expressed directed graphs:

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
text: Share common embedding H across two data matrices (e.g., RNA + ATAC cells):
code: # Define inputs inp_rna  <- factor_input(rna_matrix, "rna") inp_atac <- factor_input(atac_matrix, "atac")  # Create shared input node shared <- factor_shared(inp_rna, inp_atac)  # Build NMF layer with per-factor regularization layer <- shared |> nmf_layer(k = 10,   W = W(L1 = 0.1),   H = H(L1 = 0.05))  # Compile and fit the network net <- factor_net(   inputs = list(inp_rna, inp_atac),   output = layer,   config = factor_config(maxit = 100, seed = 42))  result <- fit(net)  # Access results result$rna$W     # RNA feature loadings (genes × 10) result$atac$W    # ATAC feature loadings (peaks × 10) result$H         # Shared cell embedding (10 × n_cells)

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
text: Stack layers hierarchical decomposition:
code: inp <- factor_input(data, "X")  # Encoder layer: reduce to 20 factors enc <- inp |> nmf_layer(k = 20, name = "encoder")  # Bottleneck: compress to 5 factors bot <- enc |> nmf_layer(k = 5, name = "bottleneck")  net <- factor_net(inputs = list(inp), output = bot,                   config = factor_config(maxit = 100, seed = 42)) result <- fit(net)

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
text: Split samples condition factorize group:
code: inp <- factor_input(data, "X") cond <- factor_condition(inp, groups = cell_types)  layer <- cond |> nmf_layer(k = 10)  net <- factor_net(inputs = list(inp), output = layer,                   config = factor_config(seed = 42)) result <- fit(net)

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
text: RcppML provides five SVD algorithms optional constraints cross-validation:
code: # Standard truncated SVD result <- svd(data, k = 10)  # PCA (centered SVD) result <- pca(data, k = 10)  # Non-negative PCA result <- pca(data, k = 10, nonneg = TRUE)  # Sparse PCA with L1 penalty result <- pca(data, k = 10, L1 = c(0, 0.1))  # Auto-rank selection result <- svd(data, k = "auto")  # Method selection result <- svd(data, k = 10, method = "lanczos")   # Fast unconstrained result <- svd(data, k = 10, method = "krylov")     # Block method, all constraints result <- svd(data, k = 10, method = "randomized") # Approximate, very fast

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
text: RcppML implements spectral clustering via recursive rank-2 NMF:
code: # Single bipartition bp <- bipartition(data) bp$samples  # Cluster assignments (0/1)  # Recursive divisive clustering clusters <- dclust(data, min_samples = 50, min_dist = 0.05) clusters$id  # Cluster labels  # Consensus clustering (multiple runs for stability) cons <- consensus_nmf(data, k = 5, n_runs = 20) plot(cons)

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
text: Project factor matrices onto new data:
code: # Given W from NMF, solve for H on new data H_new <- nnls(w = model@w, A = new_data)  # Solve for W given H (transpose projection) W_new <- nnls(h = model@h, A = new_data)  # Unconstrained LS (semi-NMF projection) H_ls <- nnls(w = model@w, A = new_data, nonneg = c(TRUE, FALSE))  # With regularization H_sparse <- nnls(w = model@w, A = new_data, L1 = c(0, 0.1))

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
text: Allow negative values W (unconstrained) keeping H non-negative:
code: model <- nmf(data, k = 10, nonneg = c(FALSE, TRUE)) any(model@w < 0)  # TRUE — W can have negative entries all(model@h >= 0)  # TRUE — H remains non-negative

title: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering
text: Sparse input: Use dgCMatrix format (Matrix package) sparse data — RcppML auto-detects uses optimized sparse routines. Solver selection: large k (> 32), solver = "cholesky" can faster coordinate descent. Use solver = "auto" (default) automatic selection. Initialization: init = "lanczos" provides better starting points can reduce iteration count 30–50%, adds upfront SVD cost. Threads: RcppML uses OpenMP. Set options(RcppML.threads = 4) control parallelism, threads = 0 available cores. GPU: matrices > 10K rows k > 8, GPU acceleration provides significant speedup. Use resource = "gpu". Streaming: datasets larger available RAM, write .spz format sp_write() factorize directly file path.

what: RcppML: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering — RcppML-package
title: RcppML: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering — RcppML-package
text: High-performance non-negative matrix factorization (NMF), singular value decomposition (SVD), divisive clustering large sparse dense matrices. Implements alternating least squares coordinate descent Cholesky NNLS solvers, diagonal scaling interpretable factors, cross-validation automatic rank selection, multiple distribution-based losses (Gaussian, Poisson, Generalized Poisson, Negative Binomial, Gamma, Inverse Gaussian, Tweedie) via iteratively reweighted least squares, regularization (L1, L2, L21, angular, graph Laplacian), optional GPU acceleration via CUDA. Includes divisive clustering via recursive rank-2 factorization, consensus clustering, SparsePress compressed sparse matrix format. Methods described DeBruine, Melcher, Triche (2021) doi:10.1101/2021.09.01.458620 .

title: RcppML: Fast Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Divisive Clustering — RcppML-package

title: Auto-select NMF distribution — auto_nmf_distribution
text: Fits NMF multiple loss functions (distributions) selects best based per-element AIC/BIC. Useful determining whether count data best modeled Gaussian (MSE), Poisson/GP, Negative Binomial loss.

title: Auto-select NMF distribution — auto_nmf_distribution
code: auto_nmf_distribution(   data,   k,   distributions = c("mse", "gp", "nb"),   criterion = c("bic", "aic"),   maxit = 50,   seed = NULL,   verbose = FALSE,   ... )

title: Auto-select NMF distribution — auto_nmf_distribution
text: data Input matrix (dense sparse dgCMatrix) k Factorization rank distributions Character vector distributions compare. Default: c("mse", "gp", "nb") criterion Selection criterion: "bic" (default) "aic" maxit Maximum iterations per fit seed Random seed reproducibility verbose Print progress comparison table ... Additional arguments passed nmf

title: Auto-select NMF distribution — auto_nmf_distribution
text: list : best Character string: name best distribution comparison Data frame distribution, nll, df, aic, bic, selected models Named list fitted nmf objects

title: Auto-select NMF distribution — auto_nmf_distribution
text: distribution, NMF fit final negative log-likelihood (NLL) computed. GP NB, C++ loss already total NLL. MSE (Gaussian), C++ loss sum squared errors (SSE), converted Gaussian NLL: \(\text{NLL} = (N/2)(1 + \log(2\pi \cdot \text{SSE}/N))\). number effective parameters : mse: \(k(m + n) + 1\) (factor params + noise variance) gp: \(k(m + n) + m\) (factor params + dispersion per row) nb: \(k(m + n) + m\) (factor params + size per row) BIC = \(2 \times \text{NLL} + \text{df} \times \log(N)\) \(N\) number observations (nonzeros sparse, entries dense). AIC = \(2 \times \text{NLL} + 2 \times \text{df}\).

title: Auto-select NMF distribution — auto_nmf_distribution
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(Matrix) set.seed(42) A <- abs(rsparsematrix(100, 50, 0.3)) result <- auto_nmf_distribution(A, k = 5) print(result$comparison) cat("Best distribution:", result$best, "\n") } # }

title: Bipartition a sample set — bipartition
text: data dense sparse matrix features rows samples columns. Prefer matrix Matrix::dgCMatrix, respectively. Also accepts file path (character string) auto-loaded based extension. tol tolerance fit (default 1e-4) nonneg enforce non-negativity rank-2 factorization used bipartitioning threads number threads OpenMP parallelization (default 0 = available) verbose print progress information (default FALSE) ... additional arguments (see Advanced Parameters section)

title: Bipartition a sample set — bipartition
text: list giving bipartition useful statistics: v       : vector giving difference sample loadings factors rank-2 factorization dist    : relative cosine distance samples within cluster centroids assigned vs. -assigned cluster size1   : number samples first cluster (positive loadings 'v') size2   : number samples second cluster (negative loadings 'v') samples1: indices samples first cluster samples2: indices samples second cluster center1 : mean feature loadings across samples first cluster center2 : mean feature loadings across samples second cluster

title: Bipartition a sample set — bipartition
text: Spectral bipartitioning popular subroutine divisive clustering. sign difference sample loadings factors rank-2 matrix factorization gives bipartition nearly identical SVD. Rank-2 matrix factorization alternating least squares faster rank-2-truncated SVD (.e. irlba). function specialization rank-2 nmf support factorization subset samples, additional calculations factorization model relevant bipartitioning. See nmf details regarding rank-2 factorization.

title: Bipartition a sample set — bipartition
text: bipartition() uses scalar nonneg (length-2) rank-2 factorizations apply constraint factors. default tol = 1e-5 (inherited nmf()'s internal rank-2 path) tighter nmf()'s global 1e-4 single rank-2 subproblems converge faster.

title: Bipartition a sample set — bipartition
text: Several parameters may specified ... argument: diag = TRUE: scale factors \(w\) \(h\) sum 1 introducing diagonal, \(d\). generally never set FALSE. Diagonalization enables symmetry models factorization symmetric matrices, convex L1 regularization, consistent factor scalings. samples = 1:ncol(): samples include bipartition, numbered 1 ncol(). Default samples. calc_dist = TRUE: calculate relative cosine distance samples within cluster either cluster centroid. TRUE, centers clusters also calculated. seed = NULL: random seed model initialization, generally needed rank-2 factorizations robust solutions recovered diag = TRUE maxit = 100: maximum number alternating updates \(w\) \(h\). Generally, rank-2 factorizations converge quickly need adjusted.

title: Bipartition a sample set — bipartition
text: Kuang, D, Park, H. (2013). "Fast rank-2 nonnegative matrix factorization hierarchical document clustering." Proc. 19th ACM SIGKDD intl. conf. Knowledge discovery data mining.

title: Bipartition a sample set — bipartition
text: Zach DeBruine

text: Run multiple NMF replicates compute consensus matrix showing co-clustering frequency samples.

title: Consensus Clustering for NMF — consensus_nmf
code: consensus_nmf(   data,   k,   reps = 50,   method = c("hard", "knn_jaccard"),   knn = 10,   seed = NULL,   threads = 0,   verbose = FALSE,   ... )

title: Consensus Clustering for NMF — consensus_nmf
text: data input matrix (samples x features clustering samples) k rank factorization reps number replicates (default 50) method consensus method: "hard" hard cluster assignments (default), "knn_jaccard" KNN-based Jaccard overlap factor loadings knn number nearest neighbors use KNN Jaccard method (default 10) seed random seed reproducibility threads number threads OpenMP parallelization (default 0 = available) verbose print progress information (default FALSE) ... additional arguments passed nmf

title: Consensus Clustering for NMF — consensus_nmf
text: List : consensus - consensus matrix (samples x samples) models - list fitted nmf objects clusters - final cluster assignments cophenetic - cophenetic correlation coefficient method - consensus method used

title: Consensus Clustering for NMF — consensus_nmf
text: Consensus clustering runs NMF multiple times different random initializations. **Hard clustering method** (method = "hard"): run, samples clustered based dominant factor W. consensus matrix C[,j] gives proportion runs samples j assigned cluster. traditional consensus clustering approach. **KNN Jaccard method** (method = "knn_jaccard"): run, k-nearest neighbors sample computed based factor loadings (W matrix). consensus matrix C[,j] average Jaccard similarity KNN sets samples j across replicates. approach robust ambiguous cluster assignments captures neighborhood structure rather hard cluster membership. High consensus values (near 1) indicate stable co-clustering neighborhood overlap. Intermediate values suggest ambiguous relationships. cophenetic correlation coefficient measures cluster stability - higher values (closer 1) indicate stable/reproducible clustering.

title: Consensus Clustering for NMF — consensus_nmf
code: # \donttest{ library(Matrix) A <- rsparsematrix(100, 50, 0.3)  # Traditional hard clustering consensus cons_hard <- consensus_nmf(A, k = 5, reps = 10, method = "hard", seed = 123)  # KNN Jaccard consensus (more robust) cons_knn <- consensus_nmf(A, k = 5, reps = 10, method = "knn_jaccard", knn = 15, seed = 123)  # Plot consensus heatmap plot(cons_hard)  plot(cons_knn)   # Check cophenetic coefficient (higher = more stable) print(cons_hard$cophenetic) #> [1] 0.901777 print(cons_knn$cophenetic) #> [1] 0.899601  # Get cluster assignments print(table(cons_hard$clusters)) #>  #>  1  2  3  4  5  #> 23 20 21 19 17  # }

Example: function(p) inputs |> nmf_layer(k = p$k, L1 = p$L1) params named list parameter vectors search . Names match arguments expected layer_fn. Default 3. strategy Search strategy: "grid" (combinations) "random" (sample n_random combinations). Default "grid". n_random Number combinations random search. Ignored grid search. Default 20. seed Seed random search sampling CV mask derivation. Default 42. verbose Print progress updates. Default TRUE.

title: Divisive clustering — dclust
text: Recursive bipartitioning rank-2 matrix factorization efficient modularity-approximate stopping criteria

title: Divisive clustering — dclust
code: dclust(   A,   min_samples,   min_dist = 0,   tol = 1e-05,   maxit = 100,   nonneg = TRUE,   seed = NULL,   threads = 0,   verbose = FALSE )

title: Divisive clustering — dclust
text: matrix features--samples sparse format (preferred class "Matrix::dgCMatrix") min_samples stopping criteria giving minimum number samples permitted cluster min_dist stopping criteria giving minimum cosine distance samples within cluster center assigned vs. unassigned cluster. 0, neither distance cluster centroids calculated. tol rank-2 NMF, correlation distance (\(1 - R^2\)) \(w\) across consecutive iterations stop factorization maxit maximum number fitting iterations (default 100) nonneg rank-2 NMF, enforce non-negativity seed random seed rank-2 NMF model initialization threads number threads OpenMP parallelization (default 0 = available) verbose print progress information (default FALSE)

title: Divisive clustering — dclust
text: list lists corresponding individual clusters: id      : character sequence "0" "1" giving position clusters along splitting hierarchy samples : 0-indexed integer indices samples cluster (add 1 R-style indexing) center  : mean feature expression samples cluster size    : number samples cluster

title: Divisive clustering — dclust
text: Divisive clustering sensitive fast method sample classification. Samples recursively partitioned two groups stopping criteria satisfied prevents successful partitioning. See nmf bipartition technical considerations optimizations relevant bipartitioning. Stopping criteria. Two stopping criteria used prevent indefinite division clusters tune clustering resolution desirable range: min_samples: Minimum number samples permitted cluster min_dist: Minimum cosine distance samples cluster center relative unassigned cluster center (approximation Newman-Girvan modularity) Newman-Girvan modularity (\(Q\)) interpretable widely used measure modularity bipartition. However, requires calculation distance within-cluster -cluster sample pairs. computationally intensive, especially large sample sets. dclust uses measure linearly approximates Newman-Girvan modularity, simply requires calculation distance samples cluster cluster centers (assigned unassigned center), orders magnitude faster compute. Cosine distance used instead Euclidean distance since handles outliers sparsity well. bipartition rejected either two clusters contains fewer min_samples mean relative cosine distance bipartition less min_dist. bipartition attempted 2 * min_samples samples cluster, meaning dist may calculated clusters. Reproducibility. rank-2 NMF approximate requires random initialization, results may vary slightly across restarts. Therefore, specify seed guarantee absolute reproducibility. setting seed, reproducibility may improved setting tol smaller number increase exactness bipartition.

title: Divisive clustering — dclust
text: dclust() uses data matrix scalar nonneg/tol parameters (matching bipartition()). default tol = 1e-5 tighter nmf()'s 1e-4 rank-2 subproblems converge faster benefit higher precision.

title: Divisive clustering — dclust
text: Schwartz, G. et al. "TooManyCells identifies visualizes relationships single-cell clades". Nature Methods (2020). Newman, MEJ. "Modularity community structure networks". PNAS (2006) Kuang, D, Park, H. (2013). "Fast rank-2 nonnegative matrix factorization hierarchical document clustering." Proc. 19th ACM SIGKDD intl. conf. Knowledge discovery data mining.

title: Divisive clustering — dclust
text: Zach DeBruine

title: Diagnose dispersion mode — diagnose_dispersion

title: Diagnose dispersion mode — diagnose_dispersion
text: data Input matrix (sparse dense) model fitted NMF model cv_threshold CV threshold declaring structured dispersion. Default 0.5. min_mu Floor predicted values. Default 1e-6.

title: Diagnose dispersion mode — diagnose_dispersion
text: list : mode Recommended DispersionMode: "global", "per_row", "per_col" global_phi Global dispersion estimate row_cv CV per-row dispersion estimates col_cv CV per-col dispersion estimates

title: Diagnose dispersion mode — diagnose_dispersion
text: Computes moment-based dispersion estimates \(\hat{\phi} = r^2 / \mu^p\) (\(p\) determined distribution) per row per column. coefficient variation (CV) per-row estimates exceeds cv_threshold, per-row dispersion recommended; similarly per-column. CVs low, global dispersion suffices.

title: Diagnose zero inflation — diagnose_zero_inflation

title: Diagnose zero inflation — diagnose_zero_inflation
text: Computes expected number zeros fitted distribution (Poisson approximation: \(P(X=0) \approx e^{-\mu}\)), compares observed zero count, recommends ZI excess large. ZI granularity determined whether per-row per-col excess rates high variance (suggesting different rows/cols different ZI levels).

title: Evaluate an NMF model — evaluate
text: x fitted model, class nmf, generally result calling nmf, models equal dimensions data ... additional development parameters data dense sparse matrix features rows samples columns. Prefer matrix Matrix::dgCMatrix, respectively. Also accepts file path (character string) auto-loaded based extension. mask dense sparse matrix values data handle missing. Alternatively, specify "zeros" "NA". missing_only calculate loss missing values specified matrix mask loss loss function use: "mse" (Mean Squared Error, default), "mae" (Mean Absolute Error), "huber" (Huber loss), "kl" (Kullback-Leibler divergence) huber_delta delta parameter Huber loss (default 1.0), ignored loss functions test_fraction fraction entries hold test set (default 0 = disabled). > 0, creates random mask test/train split. test_seed seed test set generation. NULL, attempts use test mask model's @misc$test_mask available. eval_set set evaluate: "" (default), "test" (held-entries ), "train" (non-held-entries ). used test_fraction > 0 test mask exists model. threads number threads OpenMP parallelization (default 0 = available) verbose print progress information (default FALSE)

title: Global configuration for a factorization network — factor_config
text: maxit Maximum ALS iterations per layer. Default 100. tol Convergence tolerance. Default 1e-4. loss Loss function: "mse", "mae", "huber", "kl", "gp", "nb", "gamma", "inverse_gaussian", "tweedie". Default "mse". verbose Print per-iteration diagnostics. Default FALSE. seed Random seed. Default NULL (auto). threads Number CPU threads (0 = ). Default 0. norm Normalization type: "L1", "L2", "none". Default "L1". solver NNLS solver: "auto", "cholesky", "cd". Default "auto". resource Resource override: "auto", "cpu", "gpu". Default "auto". test_fraction Fraction entries held cross-validation test set. 0 = CV (standard fit). Default 0. cv_seed Separate seed CV mask (0 = derive seed). mask_zeros TRUE, non-zero entries test set (suitable recommendation systems). FALSE, entries including zeros may appear test set. Default FALSE. patience Number iterations without test-loss improvement early stopping CV. Default 5. holdout_fraction Deprecated. Use test_fraction instead. cv_patience Deprecated. Use patience instead.

title: Fit a factorization network — fit
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(Matrix) X <- rsparsematrix(100, 50, 0.1) inp <- factor_input(X, "X") L1 <- inp |> nmf_layer(k = 5, name = "L1") net <- factor_net(inputs = inp, output = L1,                   config = factor_config(maxit = 100, seed = 42)) res <- fit(net) res$L1$W   # m x k basis matrix res$L1$H   # k x n coefficient matrix } # }

title: Create a classifier guide — guide_classifier

Positive attracts toward class centroids; negative repels. Default 1.0. side factor guide: "H" (default, sample embeddings) "W" (feature loadings).

title: nmf class methods — subset,nmf-method
text: alternating least squares matrix factorization update problem \(= wh\), updates (projection) \(h\) given equation: $$w^Twh = wA_j$$ form \(ax = b\) \(= w^Tw\) \(x = h\) \(b = wA_j\) columns \(j\) \(\). L1 penalty subtracted \(b\) generally scaled max(b), \(b = WA_j\) columns \(j\) \(\). easy way properly scale L1 penalty normalize columns \(w\) sum value (e.g. 1). scaling applied function. scaling guarantees L1 = 1 gives completely sparse solution. specializations dense sparse input matrices, symmetric input matrices, rank-1 rank-2 projections. See documentation nmf theoretical details guidance.

title: nmf class methods — subset,nmf-method
text: DeBruine, ZJ, Melcher, K, Triche, TJ. (2021). "High-performance non-negative matrix factorization large single-cell data." BioRXiv.

title: nmf class methods — subset,nmf-method
text: Zach DeBruine

title: nmf class methods — subset,nmf-method
code: # \donttest{ library(Matrix) w <- matrix(runif(1000 * 10), 1000, 10) h_true <- matrix(runif(10 * 100), 10, 100) # A is the crossproduct of "w" and "h" with 10% signal dropout mask <- rsparsematrix(1000, 100, density = 0.1) A <- (w %*% h_true) * (mask != 0) h <- nnls(w = w, A = A) cor(as.vector(h_true), as.vector(h)) #> [1] 0.3120419  # alternating projections refine solution (like NMF) h <- nnls(w = w, A = A) w <- nnls(h = h, A = A) h <- nnls(w = w, A = A) w <- nnls(h = h, A = A) h <- nnls(w = w, A = A) w <- nnls(h = h, A = A) # }

title: Non-negative matrix factorization — nmf
code: nmf(   data,   k,   tol = 1e-04,   maxit = 100,   L1 = c(0, 0),   L2 = c(0, 0),   L21 = c(0, 0),   angular = c(0, 0),   seed = NULL,   mask = NULL,   loss = c("mse", "mae", "huber", "gp", "nb", "gamma", "inverse_gaussian", "tweedie"),   huber_delta = 1,   dispersion = c("per_row", "per_col", "global", "none"),   theta_init = 0.1,   theta_max = 5,   zi = c("none", "row", "col", "twoway"),   zi_em_iters = 1L,   theta_min = 0,   nb_size_init = 10,   nb_size_max = 1e+06,   nb_size_min = 0.01,   gamma_phi_init = 1,   gamma_phi_max = 10000,   gamma_phi_min = 1e-06,   tweedie_power = 1.5,   graph_W = NULL,   graph_H = NULL,   graph_lambda = c(0, 0),   guides = NULL,   sort_model = TRUE,   mask_zeros = FALSE,   nonneg = c(TRUE, TRUE),   upper_bound = c(0, 0),   test_fraction = 0,   patience = 5,   cv_k_range = c(2, 50),   cv_seed = NULL,   track_train_loss = TRUE,   threads = 0,   verbose = FALSE,   cd_tol = 1e-08,   cd_maxit = 100L,   cd_abs_tol = 1e-15,   norm = c("L1", "L2", "none"),   streaming = "auto",   panel_cols = 0L,   resource = "auto",   projective = FALSE,   symmetric = FALSE,   solver = c("auto", "cholesky", "cd"),   init = c("random", "lanczos", "irlba"),   irls_max_iter = 5L,   irls_tol = 1e-04,   robust = FALSE,   distribution = NULL,   zero_inflation = NULL,   distribution_config = list(),   zi_config = list(),   robust_config = list(),   on_iteration = NULL,   h_init = NULL,   profile = FALSE,   dispatch = NULL,   ... )

title: Non-negative matrix factorization — nmf
text: data dense sparse matrix features rows samples columns. Prefer matrix Matrix::dgCMatrix, respectively. Also accepts file path (character string) auto-loaded based extension. k rank (integer), vector ranks cross-validation, "auto" automatic rank determination. tol tolerance fit (default 1e-4) maxit maximum number fitting iterations (default 100) L1 LASSO penalties range (0, 1], single value array length two c(w, h) L2 Ridge penalties greater zero, single value array length two c(w, h) L21 Group sparsity (L2,1-norm) penalties, single value array length two c(w, h) angular Angular regularization penalties, single value array length two c(w, h) (default c(0, 0)). seed random seed(s) initialization CV. Can : NULL random initialization, single integer reproducible initialization, vector integers multiple CV replicates, custom W matrix (k x p) custom initialization. mask dense sparse matrix values data handle missing. Alternatively, specify "zeros" "NA". loss loss function: "mse" (default), "mae", "huber", "gp", "nb". Use loss="gp" dispersion="none" KL divergence (Poisson). Use loss="nb" Negative Binomial (quadratic variance-mean, standard scRNA-seq). huber_delta delta parameter Huber loss (default 1.0). dispersion dispersion mode Generalized Poisson loss: "per_row" (default, per-feature dispersion), "global" (single shared dispersion), "none" (dispersion estimation; theta=0 reduces GP Poisson/KL divergence). theta_init initial theta value GP dispersion (default 0.1). theta_max maximum allowed theta value (default 5.0). Caps dispersion prevent instability. zi zero-inflation mode ZIGP/ZINB: "none" (default), "row" (per-row dropout), "col" (per-column dropout), "twoway" (row+column combined). Requires loss="gp" loss="nb". Estimates structural dropout probabilities pi separately count model. Currently CPU-; yet supported cross-validation mode. zi_em_iters number EM iterations per NMF iteration zero-inflation E-step M-step (default 1). iterations give accurate pi estimates per NMF step. theta_min minimum theta floor (default 0). Set > 0 prevent theta collapse zero. nb_size_init initial NB size (r) parameter Negative Binomial dispersion (default 10.0). nb_size_max maximum allowed NB size (default 1e6). nb_size_min minimum allowed NB size (default 0.01). gamma_phi_init initial dispersion parameter Gamma/Inverse Gaussian (default 1.0). gamma_phi_max maximum allowed Gamma/IG dispersion (default 1e4). gamma_phi_min minimum allowed Gamma/IG dispersion (default 1e-6). tweedie_power variance power parameter Tweedie distribution (default 1.5). V(mu) = mu^p. Special cases: p=0 Gaussian, p=1 Poisson, p=2 Gamma, p=3 Inverse Gaussian. Can also set via distribution_config$tweedie_power. graph_W sparse graph Laplacian weighted adjacency matrix (dgCMatrix, m x m) feature (row) graph regularization. Must square symmetric. Pass graph Laplacian \(L = D - \) (recommended) raw adjacency matrix; RcppML converts Laplacian internally. Controls similarity constraints W rows. graph_H sparse graph Laplacian weighted adjacency matrix (dgCMatrix, n x n) sample (column) graph regularization. Must square symmetric. Controls similarity constraints H columns. graph_lambda regularization strength graph Laplacian term, single value (applied W H graphs) c(lambda_W, lambda_H) (default c(0, 0)). Larger values enforce stronger similarity within connected nodes. guides reserved future guided/semi-supervised NMF features. sort_model TRUE (default), sort factors descending diagonal values. mask_zeros TRUE, treat zero entries missing (default FALSE). nonneg logical vector length 2 c(w, h) specifying non-negativity constraints (default c(TRUE, TRUE)). upper_bound upper bound constraints factor values, single value c(w_bound, h_bound) (default c(0, 0) = bound). test_fraction fraction entries hold cross-validation (default 0 = disabled). patience early stopping patience cross-validation (default 5). cv_k_range rank range automatic rank search, c(min, max) (default c(2, 50)). cv_seed seed(s) CV holdout pattern. track_train_loss TRUE (default), track training loss history cross-validation. threads number threads OpenMP parallelization (default 0 = available) verbose print progress information fitting (default FALSE) cd_tol relative convergence tolerance coordinate descent NNLS solver (default 1e-8). cd_maxit maximum number coordinate descent iterations NNLS solver (default 100). Acts safety cap; adaptive CD probing, effective iteration count determined automatically sample columns (typically 3-10 warm starts). cd_abs_tol absolute convergence tolerance coordinate descent (default 1e-15). norm normalization type scaling diagonal: "L1" (default), "L2", "none". Controls W H factors normalized iteration final result. streaming streaming mode large matrices: "auto", TRUE, FALSE. panel_cols number columns per streaming panel (default 0 = auto-detect). resource compute resource override: "auto" (default) auto-detects available resources, "cpu" forces CPU, "gpu" forces GPU. Can also set via RCPPML_RESOURCE environment variable (param takes priority env var). projective TRUE, use projective NMF \(H = diag(d) \cdot W^T \cdot \) instead solving H independently (default FALSE). constrains approximation column space W, yielding parts-based representations automatic sparsity coefficient matrix. symmetric TRUE, use symmetric NMF \(H = W^T\), factorizing \(\approx W \cdot d \cdot W^T\) (default FALSE). Appropriate symmetric matrices covariance, correlation, similarity matrices. W solved ; H set equal transpose W. Data square symmetric matrix. solver NNLS solver alternating least squares subproblem: "auto" (default) selects best solver given rank hardware — CD k <= 32 GPU, Cholesky otherwise. "cholesky" (Cholesky factorization + non-negativity clip, fastest high ranks), "cd" (coordinate descent, exact non-negativity enforcement, best quality low ranks). init initialization method: "random" (default, uniform random), "lanczos" (Lanczos SVD seed \(W = |U| \sqrt{\Sigma}\), \(H = |V| \sqrt{\Sigma}\); typically reduces iteration count 30-50\ (Implicitly Restarted Lanczos Bidiagonalization; alternative Lanczos high ranks k >= 32). SVD-based methods (lanczos/irlba) provide better initialization quality add overhead; use initialization quality important speed. irls_max_iter maximum IRLS iterations robust losses (default 20). irls_tol convergence tolerance IRLS weights (default 1e-4). robust robustness control. FALSE (default) robustness. TRUE Huber-type robustness delta=1.345 (95\ positive numeric value sets custom Huber delta. Works distribution: IRLS weights decomposed distribution_weight * robust_huber_modifier. distribution new unified distribution API. One : "auto" (selects via score test quick baseline fit), "gaussian" (MSE), "poisson" (GP dispersion=none), "gp" (Generalized Poisson), "nb" (Negative Binomial), "gamma" (Gamma), "inverse_gaussian" (Inverse Gaussian), "tweedie" (Tweedie continuous variance power V(mu) = mu^p; set p via tweedie_power distribution_config$tweedie_power). specified, takes precedence loss parameter. Use distribution_config set distribution-specific tuning parameters. zero_inflation zero-inflation mode (new API): "none", "row", "col", "twoway", "auto". Equivalent zi parameter preferred new API. Use zi_config additional tuning. distribution_config named list distribution-specific overrides. Supported keys: dispersion (dispersion mode), theta_init/theta_max/theta_min (GP theta), nb_size_init/nb_size_max/nb_size_min (NB size r), gamma_phi_init/gamma_phi_max/gamma_phi_min (Gamma/IG dispersion phi). zi_config named list zero-inflation overrides. Supported keys: em_iters (number EM iterations per NMF step). robust_config named list robustness overrides. Supported keys: delta (Huber delta), irls_max_iter, irls_tol. on_iteration optional callback function called NMF iteration. Receives iteration number current loss. Return FALSE stop early. h_init optional initial H matrix (k x n) custom initialization. provided alongside custom W via seed, factors initialized user-supplied values. Default NULL (auto-init). profile TRUE, enable per-iteration timing profiling. Results stored misc$profile returned object. Default FALSE. dispatch StreamPress dispatch mode .spz file input. NULL (default) "auto" automatic dispatch based available RAM; string like "inmemory", "chunked", "streaming" override. ... additional development parameters

title: Non-negative matrix factorization — nmf
text: k single integer: S4 object class nmf slots: w feature factor matrix, m x k d scaling diagonal vector length k (descending order sorting) h sample factor matrix, k x n misc list containing tol (final tolerance), iter (iteration count), loss (final loss value), loss_type (loss function used), runtime (seconds). Cross-validation models also include test_mask, test_loss, train_loss. k vector: data.frame class nmfCrossValidate columns k, rep, train_loss, test_loss, best_iter.

title: Non-negative matrix factorization — nmf
text: fast NMF implementation decomposes matrix \(\) lower-rank non-negative matrices \(w\) \(h\), columns \(w\) rows \(h\) scaled sum 1 via multiplication diagonal, \(d\): $$= wdh$$ scaling diagonal ensures convex L1 regularization, consistent factor scalings regardless random initialization, model symmetry factorizations symmetric matrices. factorization model randomly initialized.  \(w\) \(h\) updated alternating least squares. RcppML achieves high performance using Eigen C++ linear algebra library, OpenMP parallelization, dedicated Rcpp sparse matrix class, fast sequential coordinate descent non-negative least squares initialized Cholesky least squares solutions. Sparse optimization automatically applied input matrix sparse matrix (.e. Matrix::dgCMatrix). also specialized back-ends symmetric, rank-1, rank-2 factorizations. L1 penalization can used increasing sparsity factors assisting interpretability. Penalty values range 0 1, 1 gives complete sparsity. Set options(RcppML.verbose = TRUE) print model tolerances console iteration. Parallelization applied OpenMP using number threads getOption("RcppML.threads") set option(RcppML.threads = 0), example. 0 corresponds threads, let OpenMP decide.

title: Non-negative matrix factorization — nmf
text: k vector, function performs cross-validation find optimal rank: fraction (test_fraction) entries held validation Models fit rank k using training data Test loss computed held-entries Returns data.frame k, rep, train_mse, test_mse, best_iter rank/replicate Use plot() result visualize loss vs rank

title: Non-negative matrix factorization — nmf
text: loss parameter controls objective function: "mse": Mean Squared Error (default). Standard Frobenius norm minimization. "mae": Mean Absolute Error. robust outliers MSE. "huber": Huber loss. Quadratic small errors, linear large errors. Controlled huber_delta parameter. "gp": Generalized Poisson. overdispersed count data (e.g., scRNA-seq). Use dispersion control per-row global overdispersion estimation. dispersion="none", equivalent KL divergence (Poisson model). "nb": Negative Binomial. Quadratic variance-mean relationship. Standard scRNA-seq data overdispersion. Size parameter (r) estimated per-row globally. "gamma": Gamma distribution. Variance proportional mu^2. positive continuous data. Dispersion (phi) estimated via MoM Pearson estimator. "inverse_gaussian": Inverse Gaussian. Variance proportional mu^3. positive continuous data heavy right tails. "tweedie": Tweedie distribution. Variance proportional mu^p p set via tweedie_power (default 1.5). Interpolates Poisson (p=1), Gamma (p=2), Inverse Gaussian (p=3). Non-MSE losses use Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares (IRLS) may slower provides robustness outliers (MAE, Huber) better fits count data (GP, NB) positive continuous data (Gamma, Inverse Gaussian). Alternatively, use distribution parameter new unified API, maps distribution names loss functions provides distribution_config fine-grained tuning.

title: Non-negative matrix factorization — nmf
text: L21-norm regularization (also known group LASSO) encourages entire rows W columns H become zero.

title: Non-negative matrix factorization — nmf
text: Graph Laplacian regularization encourages connected nodes graph similar factor representations.

title: Non-negative matrix factorization — nmf
text: default (resource = "auto"), RcppML auto-detects available hardware. features fully supported CPU (default backend): Standard NMF (sparse dense) Cross-validation NMF loss functions (MSE, MAE, Huber, KL) regularization types (L1, L2, L21, angular, graph) Upper bound constraints OpenMP multi-threading GPU acceleration (compiled CUDA support) accelerates: Sparse NMF (standard mode) Dense NMF (standard mode) GPU experimental falls back CPU automatically unavailable. Use resource = "gpu" force GPU, resource = "cpu" force CPU. Set verbose = TRUE see backend selected.

title: Non-negative matrix factorization — nmf
text: parameter combinations currently supported. following produce error unexpected results: Cholesky + non-MSE loss Cholesky solver (solver="cholesky") support IRLS-based losses (GP, NB, Gamma, Inverse Gaussian, Tweedie). Use solver="cd" solver="auto" (default) non-MSE losses. Zero-inflation non-GP/NB losses Zero-inflation (zi zero_inflation) supported loss="gp" loss="nb". Using ZI MSE, Gamma, Inverse Gaussian, Tweedie unsupported produce incorrect results. Zero-inflation "twoway" mode zi="twoway" mode currently broken backends. Use zi="row" zi="col" instead. MAE Huber losses "mae" "huber" loss functions deprecated. Use robust parameter distribution instead (e.g., robust=TRUE distribution="gaussian"). GPU + dense CV GPU cross-validation dense matrices falls back CPU via sparseView conversion. Native GPU dense CV yet implemented. GPU + projective/symmetric CV Cross-validation projective=TRUE symmetric=TRUE falls back CPU GPU builds.

title: Non-negative matrix factorization — nmf
text: S4 methods available nmf class: predict: project NMF model (partial model) onto new samples evaluate: calculate mean squared error loss NMF model summary: data.frame giving fractional, total, mean representation factors samples features grouped criteria align: find ordering factors one nmf model best matches another nmf model prod: compute dense approximation input data sparsity: compute sparsity factor \(w\) \(h\) subset: subset, reorder, select, extract factors ([) generics dim, dimnames, t, show, head

title: Non-negative matrix factorization — nmf

title: Non-negative matrix factorization — nmf
text: Zach DeBruine

title: Non-negative matrix factorization — nmf
code: # \donttest{ # basic NMF model <- nmf(Matrix::rsparsematrix(1000, 100, 0.1), k = 10)  # cross-validation to find optimal rank library(Matrix) A <- rsparsematrix(500, 100, 0.1) cv_results <- nmf(A, k = 2:10, cv_seed = 1:3, test_fraction = 0.1) plot(cv_results)  optimal_k <- cv_results$k[which.min(cv_results$test_mse)]  # fit final model with optimal rank model <- nmf(A, optimal_k) # }
